title: ENH: Native bfloat16 (bf16) dtype support for unified memory AI workloads
body:
### Proposed new feature or change:

## Summary

As unified memory architectures become mainstream for AI inference (AMD APUs, Apple Silicon, NVIDIA Grace Hopper, upcoming DGX Spark), the lack of native bfloat16 support in numpy is causing systematic failures at GPU↔CPU boundaries.

## The Problem

Modern AI models output bfloat16 tensors for memory efficiency. On **discrete GPUs**, the explicit `.cpu()` copy across PCIe often triggers implicit dtype conversion, masking the issue. 

On **unified memory architectures**, GPU and CPU share the same memory space - no copy occurs. When code calls `.numpy()` on a bf16 tensor:

```python
# Works on discrete GPU (implicit conversion during PCIe copy)
# Crashes on unified memory (tensor already visible to CPU)
waveform = audio_tensor.squeeze(0).numpy()
```

**Error:**
```
TypeError: Got unsupported ScalarType BFloat16
```

## Affected Architectures

This impacts ALL unified memory systems:

| Platform | Status | Use Case |
|----------|--------|----------|
| AMD Strix Halo APU | Shipping now | 128GB unified, AI workstations |
| Apple Silicon M1-M4 | Shipping | Mac AI/ML workflows |
| NVIDIA Grace Hopper | Shipping | Datacenter AI |
| NVIDIA DGX Spark | Coming 2025 | Desktop AI workstation |
| Intel Arc w/ unified mem | Shipping | Consumer AI |

## Current Workaround

Every GPU→numpy boundary requires explicit conversion:

```python
# Before (breaks on unified memory)
array = tensor.numpy()

# After (works everywhere)
array = tensor.float().cpu().numpy()
```

This requires patching dozens of libraries: torchaudio, librosa, soundfile integrations, video I/O, etc.

## Real-World Impact

In a single ComfyUI AI video pipeline, I've had to patch:
- torchaudio resampler
- VibeVoice TTS output
- LatentSync lip-sync
- Wav2Lip lip-sync
- Video frame I/O

Each library assumes numpy can handle whatever dtype PyTorch gives it. On unified memory, this assumption fails.

## Proposed Solution

Add native bfloat16 dtype to numpy:

```python
import numpy as np

# Proposed
arr = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], dtype=np.bfloat16)

# Or at minimum, automatic conversion on array creation
bf16_tensor = torch.tensor([1.0], dtype=torch.bfloat16, device='cuda')
arr = bf16_tensor.numpy()  # Should auto-convert to float32 with warning
```

## Alternatives Considered

1. **Do nothing** - Force every AI library to add `.float()` calls (current state, not scalable)
2. **Warn and convert** - numpy accepts bf16, warns, converts to float32 automatically
3. **Full support** - Native bf16 dtype with arithmetic operations

Option 2 would solve 90% of the pain with minimal implementation effort.

## Context

bfloat16 was designed by Google Brain specifically for ML workloads. It's now the default output dtype for:
- Hugging Face Transformers
- PyTorch model inference
- Most modern AI models

As unified memory becomes the standard for AI hardware (it's more efficient), this gap between PyTorch's bf16 support and numpy's lack thereof will cause increasing friction.

## Environment

- numpy: 1.26.x / 2.x
- PyTorch: 2.5+ with ROCm/CUDA
- Hardware: AMD Strix Halo (gfx1151), 128GB unified memory
- OS: Ubuntu 24.04

## References

- PyTorch bfloat16: https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html
- Google Brain bfloat16: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bfloat16_floating-point_format
- AMD ROCm unified memory: https://rocm.docs.amd.com/
- NVIDIA Grace Hopper unified memory: https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/grace-hopper-superchip/

text:
ENH: Native bfloat16 (bf16) dtype support for unified memory AI workloads

### Proposed new feature or change:

## Summary

As unified memory architectures become mainstream for AI inference (AMD APUs, Apple Silicon, NVIDIA Grace Hopper, upcoming DGX Spark), the lack of native bfloat16 support in numpy is causing systematic failures at GPU↔CPU boundaries.

## The Problem

Modern AI models output bfloat16 tensors for memory efficiency. On **discrete GPUs**, the explicit `.cpu()` copy across PCIe often triggers implicit dtype conversion, masking the issue. 

On **unified memory architectures**, GPU and CPU share the same memory space - no copy occurs. When code calls `.numpy()` on a bf16 tensor:

```python
# Works on discrete GPU (implicit conversion during PCIe copy)
# Crashes on unified memory (tensor already visible to CPU)
waveform = audio_tensor.squeeze(0).numpy()
```

**Error:**
```
TypeError: Got unsupported ScalarType BFloat16
```

## Affected Architectures

This impacts ALL unified memory systems:

| Platform | Status | Use Case |
|----------|--------|----------|
| AMD Strix Halo APU | Shipping now | 128GB unified, AI workstations |
| Apple Silicon M1-M4 | Shipping | Mac AI/ML workflows |
| NVIDIA Grace Hopper | Shipping | Datacenter AI |
| NVIDIA DGX Spark | Coming 2025 | Desktop AI workstation |
| Intel Arc w/ unified mem | Shipping | Consumer AI |

## Current Workaround

Every GPU→numpy boundary requires explicit conversion:

```python
# Before (breaks on unified memory)
array = tensor.numpy()

# After (works everywhere)
array = tensor.float().cpu().numpy()
```

This requires patching dozens of libraries: torchaudio, librosa, soundfile integrations, video I/O, etc.

## Real-World Impact

In a single ComfyUI AI video pipeline, I've had to patch:
- torchaudio resampler
- VibeVoice TTS output
- LatentSync lip-sync
- Wav2Lip lip-sync
- Video frame I/O

Each library assumes numpy can handle whatever dtype PyTorch gives it. On unified memory, this assumption fails.

## Proposed Solution

Add native bfloat16 dtype to numpy:

```python
import numpy as np

# Proposed
arr = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], dtype=np.bfloat16)

# Or at minimum, automatic conversion on array creation
bf16_tensor = torch.tensor([1.0], dtype=torch.bfloat16, device='cuda')
arr = bf16_tensor.numpy()  # Should auto-convert to float32 with warning
```

## Alternatives Considered

1. **Do nothing** - Force every AI library to add `.float()` calls (current state, not scalable)
2. **Warn and convert** - numpy accepts bf16, warns, converts to float32 automatically
3. **Full support** - Native bf16 dtype with arithmetic operations

Option 2 would solve 90% of the pain with minimal implementation effort.

## Context

bfloat16 was designed by Google Brain specifically for ML workloads. It's now the default output dtype for:
- Hugging Face Transformers
- PyTorch model inference
- Most modern AI models

As unified memory becomes the standard for AI hardware (it's more efficient), this gap between PyTorch's bf16 support and numpy's lack thereof will cause increasing friction.

## Environment

- numpy: 1.26.x / 2.x
- PyTorch: 2.5+ with ROCm/CUDA
- Hardware: AMD Strix Halo (gfx1151), 128GB unified memory
- OS: Ubuntu 24.04

## References

- PyTorch bfloat16: https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html
- Google Brain bfloat16: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bfloat16_floating-point_format
- AMD ROCm unified memory: https://rocm.docs.amd.com/
- NVIDIA Grace Hopper unified memory: https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/grace-hopper-superchip/

---

The unified memory future is here. numpy's dtype support should reflect that reality.

title: BUG: Possible bug in `npy_hashtable.c`
body:
### Describe the issue:

### Bug in `PyArrayIdentityHash_GetItem()`

I think there's a bug in the new `npy_hashtable.c`, although I'm not 100% confident.

1) If `find_item()` doesn't find a matching entry, it returns the address of the empty entry where the key would be inserted.
2) `PyArrayIdentityHash_GetItem()` then calls `FT_ATOMIC_LOAD_PTR_ACQUIRE(tb_item[0])` to load the value

Normally, if the key is missing, then (2) will return `NULL`. However, if there's a concurrent write to the table, then that entry may be filled in with a *different* key and value, and the `FT_ATOMIC_LOAD_PTR_ACQUIRE(tb_item[0])` could return the incorrect value.

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/508943d1070d8f4e0712b98db2379d49304e4e38/numpy/_core/src/common/npy_hashtable.c#L306-L311

### Resizing behavior

Not exactly a bug, but I don't understand why we have logic for shrinking the table. Aren't entries only inserted, never deleted? Shrinking the table also would break the guarantees about memory usage for the `Py_GIL_DISABLED` build. Currently, it uses at most 2x the memory from saving old buckets due to doubling in size when resizing. Shrinking and growing would break this.

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/508943d1070d8f4e0712b98db2379d49304e4e38/numpy/_core/src/common/npy_hashtable.c#L202-L217

cc @kumaraditya303 @ngoldbaum 

### Reproduce the code example:

Possibly related to the crash seen in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/30653#issuecomment-3752431364


### Python and NumPy Versions:

main branch

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### How does this issue affect you or how did you find it:

See https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/30653#issuecomment-3752431364 (possibly related)

title: DOC: Behaviour of np.set_printoptions
body:
I may have misunderstand the behaviour of np.set_printoptions, so I am looking to see if the following is expected
```
import numpy as np
np.version.version
 '2.3.5'

np.get_printoptions()  # default options
{'edgeitems': 3,
 'threshold': 1000,
 'floatmode': 'maxprec',
 'precision': 8,
 'suppress': False,
 'linewidth': 75,
 'nanstr': 'nan',
 'infstr': 'inf',
 'sign': '-',
 'formatter': None,
 'legacy': False,
 'override_repr': None}

fmt_ = {"bool": lambda x: repr(x.astype(np.int32)),
      "float_kind": '{: 0.3f}'.format}  # -- change them
np.set_printoptions(
    edgeitems=10, linewidth=80, precision=3,
    floatmode='maxprec_equal',
    suppress=True,
    threshold=100,
    formatter=fmt_,
    legacy='1.25'
)  # legacy=False or legacy='1.25'

np.get_printoptions()  # -- check to see if they stick 
{'edgeitems': 3,
 'threshold': 1000,
 'floatmode': 'maxprec',
 'precision': 8,
 'suppress': False,
 'linewidth': 75,
 'nanstr': 'nan',
 'infstr': 'inf',
 'sign': '-',
 'formatter': None,
 'legacy': False,
 'override_repr': None}
```
I know about using np.printoptions as a context manager, but I am trying to see if this is expected behaviour, a recent numpy issue or my python IDE (spyder).  

Python 3.13.9, 
numpy 2.3.5

### Idea or request for content:

_No response_

title: DEP: deprecating `numpy.i0`
body:
I noticed that `numpy.i0` exists (the modified Bessel function of the first kind of order 0 [docs](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.i0.html)), which feels a bit out of place for NumPy, especially if you consider that there it's the only Bessel function that exists.
In `scipy.special` there's also an `i0`. And as far as I can tell, it doesn't rely on `numpy.i0` (it uses Cephes through [xsf](https://github.com/scipy/xsf/blob/fd4560212182caa09153fa914bc26cdec499db61/include/xsf/bessel.h#L997)). So maybe we should deprecate it in favor of `scipy.special.i0` ([docs](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.special.i0.html))?

arr = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])

flags = [inspect.BufferFlags.STRIDED, inspect.BufferFlags.READ]

barrier = Barrier(4)

    arr.__buffer__(random.choice(flags))

text:
BUG: data races in `_buffer_get_info` when multiple threads try to access buffer of ndarray

### Describe the issue:

Run the reproducer using a TSAN instrumented build of CPython and numpy.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
from threading import Thread, Barrier
import inspect
import random
arr = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])

flags = [inspect.BufferFlags.STRIDED, inspect.BufferFlags.READ]

barrier = Barrier(4)
def func():
    barrier.wait()
    arr.__buffer__(random.choice(flags))

threads = [Thread(target=func) for _ in range(4)]
for t in threads:
    t.start()
for t in threads:
    t.join()
```

title: BUG: meshgrid return type depends on the value of `copy`
body:
### Describe the issue:

`meshgrid`  is documented to return a tuple of arrays (since numpy 2.0) but seems to return a list if copy=False:



### Reproduce the code example:

```python
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: x, indexing, sparse, copy = np.array([0, 1]), 'xy', True, False

In [3]: np.meshgrid(x, indexing=indexing, sparse=sparse, copy=copy)
Out[3]: [array([0, 1])]

In [4]: np.meshgrid(indexing=indexing, sparse=sparse, copy=copy)
Out[4]: []

In [5]: np.meshgrid([1, 2], [3, 4], indexing=indexing, sparse=sparse, copy=copy)
Out[5]: 
[array([[1, 2]]),
 array([[3],
        [4]])]


In [8]: np.meshgrid([1, 2], [3, 4], indexing=indexing, sparse=sparse, copy=True)
Out[8]: 
(array([[1, 2]]),
 array([[3],
        [4]]))
```

### Error message:

```shell

```

2.5.0.dev0+git20260111.89776e2
3.12.10 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Apr 10 2025, 22:21:13) [GCC 13.3.0]

_No response_

https://github.com/cupy/cupy/pull/9582

title: BUG: os.environ manipluation during import can lead to race condition import failures
body:
### Describe the issue:

this one is twisty, bear with me as I don't know where to _start_ but I do have it figured out:

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/b91f4568c86beafb0337e33cdb982a16a2fca64e/numpy/_core/__init__.py#L84-L86

in particular does an unconditional `del os.envion[XXX]` which can fail if os.environ has been modified by another thread while this numpy import was happening.  leaving the numpy import in an incomplete state until importlib finishes unwinding as it makes an ImportError.  "hilarity" ensues if anything accesses numpy in this state (intended to be impossible for anything to get a reference to the incomplete numpy... but... rabbit hole: my rabbit hole sunk deeper and led to the creation of https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/143650 and a CPython bugfix - so it _can_ happen).

Workaround: import numpy before any thread that'd otherwise do such an import is spawned.

why is modifying os.environ tricky?  it's a global and in this case, test fixtures and tests of all forms like to do things such as mock or monkeypatch it either in its entirety or just saving and restoring its contents.  that, racing with an import that is expecting exclusive consistent access to os.environ, is non-deterministic.

if it hurts "don't do that" is valid here - but applies equally to all sides sadly. 😅 

### this is the real ask for this unusual issue:

**Can there be a better way** to plum those extension module load+init time only (right?) process environment `*BLAS_MAIN_FREE` settings into the `.multiarray` import presumably to be seen by native extension modules more directly?  I realize this is hard and fundamental perhaps to how BLAS native code "works".  But `numpy` could **instead call `os.putenv(key, "1")` and `os.unsetenv(key)`** in the cleanup to be more direct and not involve Python's `os.environ` proxy.  as I'm _hoping_ no Python level code needs to see these temporary environment variable changes in the higher level `os.environ` proxy dict right? (ever hopeful?)

### Reproduce the code example:

Hard to reproduce as it's concurrency related, but this does appear to have been the root cause until CPython's own bugfix is in and widely deployed (slow).

### Python and NumPy Versions:

Originally observed on Python 3.13.11 and numpy 2.3.5

### How does this issue affect you or how did you find it:

1. we had a pytest plugin that was spawning a thread that transitively wound up triggering a numpy import.
2. when that plugin was enabled while running pytest on code that otherwise has nothing to do with numpy and thus does not import numpy itself... it showed up as flakiness during pytest post-test teardown... because our pytest conftest.py fixture did _another import_ of something during teardown that used numpy and had a dataclass `numpy.ndarray` type annotation but occasional the numpy module would be incomplete raise a mystery `AttributeError`.

thankfully this appears to only happen in wacky CI test scenarios.  code that actually _uses_ numpy outside of tests tends to import it up front and not leave that until later sometimes from two threads at once while another diddles with the environment.

title: Memory corruption bug in Jupyter for NumPy arrays >= 2^15 elements
body:
### Describe the issue:

In a Jupyter environment, for NumPy arrays of size 2^15 elements or larger, running func(arr) can potentially corrupt the data within the array, while running func.call(arr) will not.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.ndimage import uniform_filter

def func(arr):
    x = uniform_filter(arr, size=7)
    x_before = x.copy()
    y = 2 * x * x  # This line corrupts x!
    x_after = x.copy()
    return x_before[0, 0], x_after[0, 0], y[0, 0], np.array_equal(x_before, x_after)

# Test with array size below threshold (2^15 - 1 = 32767)
small = np.ones((32767, 1))
before1, after1, y1, same1 = func(small)
before2, after2, y2, same2 = func.__call__(small)
print(f"32767 elements | func():        x_before={before1}, x_after={after1}, y={y1} | Same={same1}")
print(f"32767 elements | func.__call__: x_before={before2}, x_after={after2}, y={y2} | Same={same2}")

# Test with array size at threshold (2^15 = 32768)
large = np.ones((32768, 1))
before3, after3, y3, same3 = func(large)
before4, after4, y4, same4 = func.__call__(large)
print(f"32768 elements | func():        x_before={before3}, x_after={after3}, y={y3} | Same={same3}")
print(f"32768 elements | func.__call__: x_before={before4}, x_after={after4}, y={y4} | Same={same4}")

# Output
# 32767 elements | func():        x_before=1.0, x_after=1.0, y=2.0 | Same=True
# 32767 elements | func.__call__: x_before=1.0, x_after=1.0, y=2.0 | Same=True
# 32768 elements | func():        x_before=1.0, x_after=2.0, y=4.0 | Same=False
# 32768 elements | func.__call__: x_before=1.0, x_after=1.0, y=2.0 | Same=True
```

### Error message:

```shell

```

### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.14
numpy 2.2.6
scipy 1.16.3
ipykernel 7.1.0
ipython 9.7.0
jupyter-client 8.6.3
jupyter-core 5.9.1

### Runtime Environment:

[{'numpy_version': '2.2.6',
  'python': '3.14.0 (main, Oct 28 2025, 12:03:45) [Clang 20.1.4 ]',
  'uname': uname_result(system='Darwin', node='Users-MacBook-Pro.local', release='24.6.0', version='Darwin Kernel Version 24.6.0: Mon Jul 14 11:30:40 PDT 2025; root:xnu-11417.140.69~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T6041', machine='arm64')},
 {'simd_extensions': {'baseline': ['NEON', 'NEON_FP16', 'NEON_VFPV4', 'ASIMD'],
                      'found': ['ASIMDHP'],
                      'not_found': ['ASIMDFHM', 'SVE']}}]

### How does this issue affect you or how did you find it:

I was trying to run `skimage.metrics.structural_similarity` and the results were not making sense for very similar images. I decided to test it on the exact same image, and I would get a value of 1.0 when the image had less than 2^15 pixels, but I would get numbers like 291.26900193520385 when I increased the width of the image by one pixel, even though the exact same image was being used twice in the input. This kind of jump made no sense, so I dug deeper and found this minimal reproducible case of the memory being corrupted.

This kind of jump made no sense, so I dug deeper and found this minimal reproducible case of the memory being corrupted.

title: BUG: Memory leak in np.eye
body: ### Describe the issue: `np.eye` leaks memory in `numpy==2.4.0` (~130 MB in 50k calls). After [`memray`](https://github.com/bloomberg/memray)-ing (both with `pymalloc` and `malloc` as allocators), the culprit seems to be this line in `eye` definition. ```m[:M-k].flat[i::M+1] = 1``` The leak is not present in `numpy==2.3.2`. memray memory graph for the example code below ### Reproduce the code example: ```python import numpy as np print(np.__version__) for _ in range(50_000): a = np.eye(4) ``` ### Error message: ```shell ``` ### Python and NumPy Versions: 2.4.0 3.14.2 | packaged by Anaconda, Inc. | (main, Dec 19 2025, 11:49:32) [GCC 14.3.0] ### Runtime Environment: [{'numpy_version': '2.4.0', 'python': '3.14.2 | packaged by Anaconda, Inc. | (main, Dec 19 2025, ' '11:49:32) [GCC 14.3.0]', 'uname': uname_result(system='Linux', node='laptop', release='6.14.0-37-generic', version='#37~24.04.1-Ubuntu SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Thu Nov 20 10:25:38 UTC 2', machine='x86_64')}, {'simd_extensions': {'baseline': ['X86_V2'], 'found': ['X86_V3'], 'not_found': ['X86_V4', 'AVX512_ICL', 'AVX512_SPR']}}, {'ignore_floating_point_errors_in_matmul': False}, {'architecture': 'Haswell', 'filepath': '/home/user/miniconda3/envs/eyeleak/lib/python3.14/site-packages/numpy.libs/libscipy_openblas64_-fdde5778.so', 'internal_api': 'openblas', 'num_threads': 14, 'prefix': 'libscipy_openblas', 'threading_layer': 'pthreads', 'user_api': 'blas', 'version': '0.3.30'}] ### How does this issue affect you or how did you find it: While loading vertices info from a `.glb` file, we were doing many `matrix = np.eye(4, dtype=np.float32)` and getting OoM errors. title: ENH: Requist for method `ndarray.abs()`
body:
### Proposed new feature or change:

There needs to have a method `ndarray.abs()`. For example, let `x = numpy.array([-1, 0, 2])`, very often I need to compute elementwise absolute value like `x.abs()` whose expected result is `numpy.array([1, 0, 2])`. But until now I need to write `numpy.abs(x)`, which is not so convenient. I believe many people want this feature. Actually, I wait this method for more than ten years, but it still has not appear yet. So I decide to report this requist.

title: DOC: Add python-level parallelism tips to the docs
body:
### Issue with current documentation:

Right now the docs don't really have any suggestions on how to write performant code with NumPy. With the free-threaded build, there's increasing pressure to support multithreaded use but both multiprocessing and multithreading have seen lots of use with NumPy out in the real world for a long time.

I guess we could also tell people "just use dask!" and maybe these docs should suggest dask but I also think documenting how to work with standard library parallelism primitives is important.

Also, while working on https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/30494 we've been assembling some knowledge about ways to improve performance under multithreading. We need a place to add these tips in a way that's visible to users.

### Idea or request for content:

A new section in the user guide with content about how to use multiple CPU cores to work with numpy arrays. It probably makes sense to cover both multithreading and multiprocessing.

This can include both general guidance for writing performant code as well as tips for micro-optimizations like I point out here: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/30514#issuecomment-3716554540.

I think it should also probably include general tips about how to write parallel code, including advising to minimize shared state and how to avoid contention around shared state with threads or lots of overhead due to pickle with multiprocessing.

title: numpy ma masked_equal returns ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence. when fill_value is float and missing_value is np.ndarray: setting anything but 0-dim array to scalar not allowed anymore in 2.4.0
body:
### Describe the issue:

Hi folks, not sure if a bug - at any rate, a rather peculiar case: given a masked array, that has a fill_value, numpy `ma.masked_equal` fails when passing an ndarray (single element) for missing_value, works fine if float; this is new behaviour for numpy 2.4.0. Cheers very much! 🍺 

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np


# works fine
missing_value = 33.
data = np.ma.array(
    [[[-900., 33.], [33., -900], [33., 44.]]],
    mask=False,
    fill_value=-900.0,
    dtype=float
)
s = np.ma.masked_equal(data, missing_value)
print(s)

# fails only with numpy=2.4.0
missing_value = np.array([33.], dtype=float)
data = np.ma.array(
    [[[-900., 33.], [33., -900], [33., 44.]]],
    mask=False,
    fill_value=-900.0,
    dtype=float
)
s = np.ma.masked_equal(data, missing_value)
print(s)
```

### Error message:

```shell
TypeError: only 0-dimensional arrays can be converted to Python scalars

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/valeriu/numpy_mre.py", line 23, in <module>
    s = np.ma.masked_equal(data, missing_value)
  File "/home/valeriu/miniconda3/envs/pyactive5/lib/python3.14/site-packages/numpy/ma/core.py", line 2161, in masked_equal
    output.fill_value = value
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/valeriu/miniconda3/envs/pyactive5/lib/python3.14/site-packages/numpy/ma/core.py", line 3850, in fill_value
    _fill_value[()] = target
    ~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^
ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.
```

### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.14 and numpy 2.4.0

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### How does this issue affect you or how did you find it:

All fine, we fixed it so we use only floats for `missing_value` but others may be affected.

title: BUG: Return type changed in percentile for higher precision `q`
body:
gh-29105 incorrectly and unintentionally changed the return type for some percentile operations:
```
np.percentile(np.ones(10, dtype="float32"), np.float64(3))
```
should have the `q` float64 ensure a float64 result type, but doesn't on account of incorrectly assuming that the arrays dtype trumps the result one in the old code.
The part pertaining to using a higher precision internally for `q` itself should be fine though.

_Originally posted by @seberg in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/29105#issuecomment-3714550625_

title: BUG: RNG spawn() with negative argument causes intermittent exception
body:
### Describe the issue:

The `spawn` method of a `numpy.random.Generator` instance is used to create child generators. It takes a single integer parameter giving the number of child generators to spawn. If this integer is negative (which, to my mind, is an invalid value) the behaviour is inconsistent, sometimes returning an empty list and sometimes raising an OverflowError with a message that is not obviously linked to the parameter being invalid. 

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
# This cannot run as a file due to the exceptions, but running
# line-by-line in an interactive session shows the problem.

import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng()
rng.spawn(1)
rng.spawn(-1)  # returns empty list
rng.spawn(-1)  # error
rng.spawn(2)   # seems to reset something
rng.spawn(-1)  # empty list
rng.spawn(-1)  # empty list
rng.spawn(-1)  # error

# The number of positive spawns seems to need to be offset by
# negative spawns, e.g., if you run rng.spawn(3) then you can
# run rng.spawn(-1) three times with no exception, and the
# fourth rng.spawn(-1) causes an exception. You can also run
# rng.spawn(3) followed by rng.spawn(-3) with no exception,
# and then the next negative spawn will fail.
```

### Error message:

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last)

File numpy/random/_generator.pyx:299, in numpy.random._generator.Generator.spawn()

File numpy/random/bit_generator.pyx:632, in numpy.random.bit_generator.BitGenerator.spawn()

File numpy/random/bit_generator.pyx:486, in numpy.random.bit_generator.SeedSequence.spawn()

OverflowError: can't convert negative value to uint32_t
```

### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.3.5
3.13.11 (main, Dec  7 2025, 13:01:45) [GCC 15.2.1 20251112]

### Runtime Environment:

[{'numpy_version': '2.3.5',
  'python': '3.13.11 (main, Dec  7 2025, 13:01:45) [GCC 15.2.1 20251112]',
  'uname': uname_result(system='Linux', node='e1005-133', release='6.18.1-arch1-2', version='#1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Sat, 13 Dec 2025 18:23:21 +0000', machine='x86_64')},
 {'simd_extensions': {'baseline': ['SSE', 'SSE2', 'SSE3'],
                      'found': ['SSSE3',
                                'SSE41',
                                'POPCNT',
                                'SSE42',
                                'AVX',
                                'F16C',
                                'FMA3',
                                'AVX2'],
                      'not_found': ['AVX512F',
                                    'AVX512CD',
                                    'AVX512_SKX',
                                    'AVX512_CLX',
                                    'AVX512_CNL',
                                    'AVX512_ICL',
                                    'AVX512_SPR']}},
 {'ignore_floating_point_errors_in_matmul': False},
 {'filepath': '/usr/lib/libgomp.so.1.0.0',
  'internal_api': 'openmp',
  'num_threads': 24,
  'prefix': 'libgomp',
  'user_api': 'openmp',
  'version': None}]

### How does this issue affect you or how did you find it:

For some code I am working on, I have a pool of child generators (I need repeatable sequences for some simulations). When one is requested, the code checks if it has been spawned and if not does so, effectively something like:

```python
N_missing = N_needed - len(pool)
if N_missing > 0:
    pool.extend(master_rng.spawn(N_missing))
```

I had a bug where I was using `if N_missing:` instead of `if N_missing > 0`, and so was sometimes getting these error messages. The fact `spawn` sometimes returned an empty list made this behaviour intermittent, and the exception message made it more difficult to track down.

To be clear, this was observed due to a bug in my code and I don't expect `spawn()` to support negative inputs. I do I think that an `n_children > 0` (or `n_children >= 0` if zero should be allowed) check raising an exception with a clear message would be beneficial. I am not sure if this should be implemented in `Generator.spawn` or at a lower level (`BitGenerator.spawn` / `SeedSequence.spawn`), or in all of them.

title: ENH: pickling of memmap results in copy of data
body:
### Proposed new feature or change:

When using `multiprocessing`, if a `memmap` object is one of the parameters passed to the processes, the file _contents_ are pickled, not information necessary to memory map the file again.

It would allow for much better operation (and performance) if the unpickling recreated the actual memory map.

title: ENH: equal_mask flag for assert_array_equal
body:
### Proposed new feature or change:

Working with masked arrays, unit tests often need to check if a function returns the expected  masked array.
However, doing it with assert_array_equal is error prone because masked dimensions will not be compared.
Hence this will succeed:
```
ret = tested_function() # suppose returned np.ma.MaskedArray([4.0], mask=[True])
assert_array_equal(ret, np.array([3.0]))
```

May an `equal_mask` flag could be added so that the function compares that, for each item, both or none are masked

title: "drop" fails by default when label to drop is not in the index, but "rename" silently passes by. Wouldn't it make sense to synchronize?
body:
#### Is your feature request related to a problem?

.drop and .rename have similar signatures to some extent, and implement basic dataframe functionalities. When drop is called and the label is not in the index, it fails by default (due to the errors='raise' default parameter). However, when rename is called with a label not in the specified index, it passes by silently by default. I think it would be a useful improvement to get the parameterization and default behavior of these functions aligned, possibly by enriching rename. I know it might be incompatible with previous versions if it would start failing if a label is not in the index, but I am also concerned if it is an intentional and valid usecase to rename columns which might not be present in the dataframe.

#### Describe the solution you'd like

rename should be extended with the same parameters as drop, possibly with the default setting of raising errors.

#### API breaking implications

If the raising of errors is not made default, it should not break anything. If the raising of errors is default (making it similar to drop), then those codes attempt to rename labels not present in the index would fail. Possibly it could be a 2 stage improvement, first raising a warning and in a later release raising an error.

#### Describe alternatives you've considered

One can do workarounds in the host code, but I don't see alternatives solutions.

#### Additional context

I came across this issue when working with data as part of my job. Columns which I expected at some point of the code were not there, the reason was that I did not specify the index='columns' flag in rename, which passed by silently trying to rename entries in the row index. If rename would have failed as drop would have, it would have been easier to figure out where is the issue. 

Then I was pondering if it is a valid use case to pass by silently if there is no label to be renamed, and decided to write this request.

```python
>>> pandas.__version__
'1.3.0.dev0+1040.g454194e76a'
>>> tmp= pandas.DataFrame({'a': [1, 2, 3], 'b': [3, 2, 1]}, index=['b', 'c', 'd'])
>>> tmp
   a  b
b  1  3
c  2  2
d  3  1
>>> tmp.drop(['e'], axis='columns')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/pandas/pandas/core/frame.py", line 4776, in drop
    return super().drop(
  File "/home/pandas/pandas/core/generic.py", line 4211, in drop
    obj = obj._drop_axis(labels, axis, level=level, errors=errors)
  File "/home/pandas/pandas/core/generic.py", line 4246, in _drop_axis
    new_axis = axis.drop(labels, errors=errors)
  File "/home/pandas/pandas/core/indexes/base.py", line 5925, in drop
    raise KeyError(f"{labels[mask]} not found in axis")
KeyError: "['e'] not found in axis"
>>> tmp
   a  b
b  1  3
c  2  2
d  3  1
>>> tmp.rename({'e': 'g'}, axis='columns')
   a  b
b  1  3
c  2  2
d  3  1
>>> tmp
   a  b
b  1  3
c  2  2
d  3  1
>>>
```
Any comments are welcome!

title: BUG: `Index` accepts lists as values
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
>>> import pandas as pd
>>> ind=pd.Index([["str", "rts"]])
>>> ind
Index([['str', 'rts']], dtype='object')
```

### Issue Description

It seems that `Index` accepts a list of list of strings, but that's not hashable, and docs say that we only accept hashable values in an Index.


### Expected Behavior

This should be rejected during construction.

title: BUG: regression in nightlies: `Index.str.findall` crashes
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

for cls in (pd.Series, pd.Index):
    print(cls)
    idx = cls(['a', 'aaa', 'abcd', 'b'])
    out = idx.str.findall("a")
    print(out)
```

### Issue Description

The above snippet works with pandas 3.0.2 and crashes with `3.1.0.dev0+830.g711e8a7aad`.

### Expected Behavior

Pandas 3.0.2 output:
```
<class 'pandas.Series'>
0          [a]
1    [a, a, a]
2          [a]
3           []
dtype: object
<class 'pandas.Index'>
Index([['a'], ['a', 'a', 'a'], ['a'], []], dtype='object')
```
Pandas 3.1.0.dev0+830.g711e8a7aad output:
```
<class 'pandas.Series'>
0          [a]
1    [a, a, a]
2          [a]
3           []
dtype: object
<class 'pandas.Index'>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "demo.py", line 6, in <module>
    out = idx.str.findall("a")
  File "pandas/core/strings/accessor.py", line 142, in wrapper
    return func(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "pandas/core/strings/accessor.py", line 3329, in findall
    return self._wrap_result(result, returns_string=False)
           ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "pandas/core/strings/accessor.py", line 372, in _wrap_result
    return Index(result, name=name, dtype=dtype, copy=False)
  File "pandas/core/indexes/base.py", line 582, in __new__
    lib.check_all_hashable(arr)
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^
  File "pandas/_libs/lib.pyx", line 2628, in pandas._libs.lib.check_all_hashable
TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'
```

### Installed Versions

pandas nightly wheels `3.1.0.dev0+830.g711e8a7aad`
pyarrow 23.0

body:
```
>>> midx1 = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([["a", "b", "c"], [1, 2]])
>>> midx2 = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([["a", "b", "c"], [1, 2]])
>>> from pandas.core.indexes.frozen import FrozenList
>>> midx2._codes = FrozenList([midx2.codes[0].astype("int64"), midx2.codes[1]])
>>> midx1.equals(midx2)
```

title: BUG: Data corruption after `.eval(..., inplace=True)`.
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
nums = rng.choice([-1, 1], size=10_000).astype(np.int32)
df = pd.DataFrame({"nums": nums.copy()})
df.eval("nums2 = nums", inplace=True)
mask = rng.random(10_000) > 0.5
df.loc[mask, "nums2"] = 0
assert np.array_equal(df["nums"].to_numpy(), nums), "BUG: nums was corrupted!"
```

### Issue Description

When using `df.eval("new=old",inplace=True)` to initialize a column with another column, no genuine copy is made, leading to data corruption when modifying column entries through `df.loc`. This affects pandas starting from 3.0.0 up to the latest version 3.0.3.

### Expected Behavior

Assert should pass. Pandas should always create a data copy when initializing with `df.eval("new=old", inplace=True)`. Writes to the new column through `df.loc[]` should not affect the source column.

BUG: Data corruption after `.eval(..., inplace=True)`.

### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
nums = rng.choice([-1, 1], size=10_000).astype(np.int32)
df = pd.DataFrame({"nums": nums.copy()})
df.eval("nums2 = nums", inplace=True)
mask = rng.random(10_000) > 0.5
df.loc[mask, "nums2"] = 0
assert np.array_equal(df["nums"].to_numpy(), nums), "BUG: nums was corrupted!"
```

### Issue Description

When using `df.eval("new=old",inplace=True)` to initialize a column with another column, no genuine copy is made, leading to data corruption when modifying column entries through `df.loc`. This affects pandas starting from 3.0.0 up to the latest version 3.0.3.

### Expected Behavior

Assert should pass. Pandas should always create a data copy when initializing with `df.eval("new=old", inplace=True)`. Writes to the new column through `df.loc[]` should not affect the source column.

title: PERF: Replace dateutil-based parser fallback with a regex-based parser in tslibs.parsing
body:
In non-ISO8601 cases we use dateutil's parser, but this is 1) not-optimized pure-python and 2) parses some strings that we probably should raise on, e.g.:

"Jan"
"10am"
"5th"
"1234"

The hard part here is deciding exactly what cases we want to allow and a deprecation cycle for cases that we disallow.

Using a regex with a fixed set of formats would be significantly faster (preliminary benchmarks are 5-15x on cases with month name sand 100x on cases with "AM" or "PM").

Running list of issues that this might address: #17265

title: BUG: pyarrow-nightly CI fails during Pixi install due to stale locked nightly wheel
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
bash
pixi install -e pyarrow-nightly --locked
Error: The process '/home/runner/.pixi/bin/pixi' failed with exit code 1
```

### Issue Description

The pyarrow-nightly environment uses the scientific-python nightly wheel index. However, pixi.lock pins a direct URL for the pyarrow-25.0.0.dev48 wheel, and that nightly artifact has been removed from the index, causing a 404 during installation.

### Expected Behavior

The pyarrow-nightly CI job should not fail simply because an older nightly wheel artifact has been pruned.

### Installed Versions

title: ENH: read_json() should work with single entry json {"refmet_name":"Citric acid","formula":"C6H8O7"}'
body:
### Feature Type

- [ ] Adding new functionality to pandas

- [X] Changing existing functionality in pandas

- [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas


### Problem Description

There are APIs that return single entry JSON data.

https://www.metabolomicsworkbench.org/rest/refmet/match/CITRATE/name/
>>> '{"refmet_name":"Citric acid","formula":"C6H8O7","exactmass":"192.0270","super_class":"Organic acids","main_class":"TCA acids","sub_class":"TCA acids"}'

    url = 'https://www.metabolomicsworkbench.org/rest/refmet/match/CITRATE/name/'
    pd.read_json(url)
    >>>ValueError: If using all scalar values, you must pass an index

`pd.read_json()` does not handle this well as it expects a list.

It would work with this string: `'[{"refmet_name":"Citric acid","formula":"C6H8O7","exactmass":"192.0270","super_class":"Organic acids","main_class":"TCA acids","sub_class":"TCA acids"}]'`

It would be nice if read_json would handle this special case of a single entry. 

### Feature Description

```
text = '{"refmet_name":"Citric acid","formula":"C6H8O7","exactmass":"192.0270","super_class":"Organic acids","main_class":"TCA acids","sub_class":"TCA acids"}'
pd.read_json(text)
```

should be equivalent to

```
text = '[{"refmet_name":"Citric acid","formula":"C6H8O7","exactmass":"192.0270","super_class":"Organic acids","main_class":"TCA acids","sub_class":"TCA acids"}]'
pd.read_json(text)
```

No new function is required.

### Alternative Solutions

...

### Additional Context

_No response_

title: ENH/API: Architectural Audit - Are other inplace=True methods bypassing CoW reference tracking?
body:
### Feature Type

- [x] Adding new functionality to pandas

- [ ] Changing existing functionality in pandas

- [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas


### Problem Description

### Background
While investigating a memory corruption issue yesterday (reported in #65664  and patched in PR #65675 ), I found a structural vulnerability in how the computation engine interacts with Pandas 3.0's Copy-on-Write (CoW) architecture. 

Specifically, `df.eval("new=old", inplace=True)` was bypassing CoW protections. The `eval` engine reached into the `BlockManager`, extracted the raw data arrays, evaluated them, and handed them back. Because the data took a detour through the external computation engine, the CoW reference tracking metadata was stripped. The DataFrame assumed the returned array was unshared memory, leading to silent memory corruption when the alias was later modified.

### The Architectural Question
My patch forces an explicit `.copy()` before assignment if `inplace=True` inside `pandas.core.computation.eval`. However, this raises a much larger architectural question: **Are there other C-engine, Cython, or NumExpr-routed methods that are secretly leaking CoW tracking data when `inplace=True` is invoked?**

### Proposed Audit Targets
We know that standard `__setitem__` handles CoW perfectly. But any method that extracts raw arrays, computes externally, and attempts to write back `inplace` is at risk. I propose a systematic audit of the following `inplace=True` methods to ensure they aren't making the same tracking error:

1. **`DataFrame.query(inplace=True)`:** Since this relies on the exact same underlying `pandas.core.computation` engines (`numexpr` / `python`) as `eval`, it is the most likely candidate for a parallel vulnerability.
2. **`DataFrame.where()` & `DataFrame.mask()`:** When backed by external engines or fast-path Cython blocks.
3. **`DataFrame.fillna()` & `DataFrame.replace()`:** Specifically when operating on mixed dtypes that might force a block consolidation or split during the inplace write.
4. **`DataFrame.clip()`**

### Action Items
Rather than playing whack-a-mole with memory bugs, I suggest we:
1. Identify all `inplace=True` paths that route through `numexpr` or bypass the standard `NDFrame.__setitem__` API.
2. Implement a standardized CoW "alias corruption" test suite specifically targeting these external engine routes.
3. Determine if we need a centralized validation wrapper that explicitly checks reference counts when C-engines return data for inplace assignment.

I am happy to lead the audit on `.query()` and `.where()` to start, but wanted to bring this to the core team's attention to see if there is already a broader strategy for validating `inplace` safety under CoW. Thoughts?

### Feature Description

I propose implementing a two-phase solution to secure the CoW perimeter:

**1. A Centralized Testing Utility:**
Introduce a new test utility in `pandas/tests/copy_view/` specifically designed to test external-engine mutations. 
Pseudocode:
```python
def assert_cow_inplace_safe(df, operation_func):
    """
    Ensures that an inplace operation does not corrupt aliases 
    if it routes through an external engine.
    """
    df_copy = df.copy()
    alias = df["column_name"]
    
    # Perform inplace operation that might strip CoW tracking
    operation_func(df) 
    
    # Mutate the alias
    alias.iloc[0] = modified_value
    
    # If the original dataframe was mutated, the CoW perimeter failed.
    assert_frame_equal(df, df_copy) 
```

.........
2. A C-Engine Return Wrapper:
If the audit finds multiple leaks in .query(), .where(), etc., rather than patching them individually, we should implement a validation layer inside BlockManager.setitem_inplace (or the equivalent API) that explicitly asserts: if pd.options.mode.copy_on_write: assert result.refs is safe before mapping the C-engine memory back to the dataframe.



### **Alternative Solutions**
**Alternative 1: The "Whack-a-Mole" Approach (Status Quo)**
We do nothing structurally, and wait for users to report data corruption bugs one by one as they upgrade to Pandas 3.0. This risks user trust in the new CoW architecture.

**Alternative 2: Do nothing and wait for PDEP-8**
Since `inplace=True` is broadly slated for deprecation and removal in Pandas 4.0 (PDEP-8), we could argue that investing in `inplace` infrastructure isn't worth the time. *However*, because users will be relying on Pandas 3.x during the CoW transition period, allowing silent data corruption to exist during this window is highly dangerous. We need to secure the perimeter until `inplace` is officially removed.

### Additional Context

This architectural concern was triggered by my investigation into Issue #65664, where I successfully patched a live CoW tracking bypass in `DataFrame.eval(inplace=True)` via PR #65675.

Introduce a new test utility in specifically designed to test external-engine mutations.
Pseudocode:

.........
2. A C-Engine Return Wrapper:
If the audit finds multiple leaks in .query(), .where(), etc., rather than patching them individually, we should implement a validation layer inside BlockManager.setitem_inplace (or the equivalent API) that explicitly asserts: if pd.options.mode.copy_on_write: assert result.refs is safe before mapping the C-engine memory back to the dataframe.

Alternative Solutions
Alternative 1: The "Whack-a-Mole" Approach (Status Quo)
We do nothing structurally, and wait for users to report data corruption bugs one by one as they upgrade to Pandas 3.0. This risks user trust in the new CoW architecture.

Alternative 2: Do nothing and wait for PDEP-8
Since is broadly slated for deprecation and removal in Pandas 4.0 (PDEP-8), we could argue that investing in infrastructure isn't worth the time. *However*, because users will be relying on Pandas 3.x during the CoW transition period, allowing silent data corruption to exist during this window is highly dangerous. We need to secure the perimeter until is officially removed.

Additional Context

This architectural concern was triggered by my investigation into Issue #65664, where I successfully patched a live CoW tracking bypass in via PR #65675.

title: `groupby().first()` docs should explain distinction between nth and first
body:
### Problem description

The existing doc for `groupby().first()` (https://pandas-docs.github.io/pandas-docs-travis/reference/api/pandas.core.groupby.GroupBy.first.html?highlight=first#pandas.core.groupby.GroupBy.first) does not describe the behavior with respect to missing data.  In particular, it does not mention the fact that the behavior is broadcasting columnwise.

The docs read: "Compute first of group values...Computed first of values within each group."   I think the correct description is "For each column, compute the first non-null entry, possibly aggregating values from across multiple rows."  We might also want a simple example to explain the behavior.  

#### Code Sample, a copy-pastable example if possible

```python
import pandas as pd
x = pd.DataFrame(dict(A=[1, 1, 3], B=[None, 5, 6], C=[1, 2, 3]))
print(x.groupby("A", as_index=False).first())
print(x.groupby("A", as_index=False).nth(0))
print(x.groupby("A", as_index=False).head(1))
[...]
   A    B  C
0  1  5.0  1
1  3  6.0  3
   A    B  C
0  1  NaN  1
2  3  6.0  3
   A    B  C
0  1  NaN  1
2  3  6.0  3
```

title: Feature request: read_excel to support hyperlinks split into label and hyperlink
body:
Situation:
.xlsx file having a column with label and hyperlinks (see screenshot collage)
In Excel, label: Python (that has a hyperlink to i.e.: www.python.org)
#### Code Sample, a copy-pastable example if possible

data = pd.read_excel('Book1.xlsx')
column has just "Python" as content. Hyperlink information is lost.
(see screenshot)

![excel-hyperlink](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/19929980/16044286/25e27eca-3244-11e6-8ce4-2ae0ec34ba77.png)
#### Expected Output, Proposal:

Add an argument  for example named hyperlinkparser to read_excel()
that offers options: "label", "hyperlink", "both"
label ... just the label (this is how it is handled now)
hyperlink ... just the hyperlink, drop the label
split ... created two separate columns for label and hyperlink each

with example it could look like:
         | MyLinks | MyLinks_hyperlink
0       | Python   | www.python.org

btw. openpyxl 2.4.+ has this feature now included (was broken in previous versions). Maybe this helps for implementation.

Thank you!

title: ENH: Add `df.corrupt()` accessor to inject random faults and typos for Data Science education
body:
### Feature Type

- [x] Adding new functionality to pandas

- [ ] Changing existing functionality in pandas

- [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas


### Problem Description

In Data Science and AI education, teaching students how to clean and preprocess "dirty data" (handling missing values, detecting outliers, removing duplicates, and fixing text typos) is a critical skill. 

Currently, professors and students struggle to find realistic, messy datasets for practice. When using clean benchmark datasets (like Iris or Titanic), users have to write long, repetitive custom scripts from scratch just to artificially mess up the data for testing and educational purposes. There is no built-in, one-stop tool in Pandas to inject controlled chaos into a DataFrame.


### Feature Description

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import random
import string

@pd.api.extensions.register_dataframe_accessor("corrupt")
class CorruptAccessor:
    """
    A vectorized Pandas DataFrame accessor to inject synthetic anomalies, 
    missing values, outliers, duplicates, and smart human-like typos 
    for educational and pipeline testing purposes.
    """
    def __init__(self, pandas_obj):
        self._obj = pandas_obj

    def _resolve_count(self, param, total_elements):
        """Helper to support both exact integer counts and float percentages."""
        if isinstance(param, float) and 0.0 <= param <= 1.0:
            return int(param * total_elements)
        elif isinstance(param, (int, np.integer)):
            return int(param)
        else:
            raise TypeError("Parameters must be either an int (count) or a float between 0.0 and 1.0 (ratio).")

    def __call__(self, num_nan=0, num_outliers=0, num_duplicates=0, num_typos=0, random_state=None):
        if random_state is not None:
            np.random.seed(random_state)
            random.seed(random_state)

        # Work on a deep copy to guarantee immutability of the source dataset
        df = self._obj.copy()
        num_rows, num_cols = df.shape
        total_cells = num_rows * num_cols
        
        if num_rows == 0 or num_cols == 0:
            return df

        # --- 1. Resolve and Validate Injection Targets ---
        n_nan = self._resolve_count(num_nan, total_cells)
        if n_nan > total_cells:
            raise ValueError(f"Resolved NaNs ({n_nan}) cannot exceed total cells ({total_cells}).")

        numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns
        total_numeric_cells = num_rows * len(numeric_cols)
        n_outliers = self._resolve_count(num_outliers, total_numeric_cells)
        if n_outliers > total_numeric_cells:
            raise ValueError(f"Resolved outliers ({n_outliers}) cannot exceed total numeric cells ({total_numeric_cells}).")

        n_duplicates = self._resolve_count(num_duplicates, num_rows)
        
        text_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['object', 'string']).columns
        total_text_cells = num_rows * len(text_cols)
        n_typos = self._resolve_count(num_typos, total_text_cells)
        if n_typos > total_text_cells:
            raise ValueError(f"Resolved typos ({n_typos}) cannot exceed total text cells ({total_text_cells}).")

        # --- 2. Vectorized Fault Injection Pipelines ---

        # Pipeline A: Global Vectorized NaN Injection
        if n_nan > 0:
            flat_indices = np.random.choice(total_cells, size=n_nan, replace=False)
            nan_mask = np.zeros(total_cells, dtype=bool)
            nan_mask[flat_indices] = True
            nan_mask = nan_mask.reshape(num_rows, num_cols)
            
            for i, col in enumerate(df.columns):
                col_mask = nan_mask[:, i]
                if col_mask.any():
                    df.iloc[col_mask, i] = np.nan

        # Pipeline B: Statistically Robust Outlier Injection (Vectorized per Column)
        if n_outliers > 0 and len(numeric_cols) > 0:
            flat_num_indices = np.random.choice(total_numeric_cells, size=n_outliers, replace=False)
            outlier_mask = np.zeros(total_numeric_cells, dtype=bool).reshape(num_rows, len(numeric_cols))
            outlier_mask.ravel()[flat_num_indices] = True
            
            for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
                col_mask = outlier_mask[:, i]
                if col_mask.any():
                    std_dev = df[col].std()
                    if pd.isna(std_dev) or std_dev == 0:
                        std_dev = 1.0
                    df.loc[col_mask, col] = (df.loc[col_mask, col] * 3) + (5 * std_dev)

        # Pipeline C: Vectorized Duplicate Injection
        if n_duplicates > 0:
            duplicated_rows = df.sample(n=n_duplicates, replace=True, random_state=random_state)
            df = pd.concat([df, duplicated_rows], ignore_index=True)

        # Pipeline D: Smart Realistic Text Typo Injection
        if n_typos > 0 and len(text_cols) > 0:
            flat_text_indices = np.random.choice(total_text_cells, size=n_typos, replace=False)
            typo_mask = np.zeros(total_text_cells, dtype=bool).reshape(num_rows, len(text_cols))
            typo_mask.ravel()[flat_text_indices] = True
            
            def _mutate_string(val):
                """Helper to simulate realistic human typing errors dynamically."""
                s = str(val)
                if not s or s == "nan":
                    return s
                
                # Randomly pick an error type: deletion, substitution, or insertion
                operation = random.choice(['delete', 'substitute', 'insert'])
                random_char = random.choice(string.ascii_lowercase)
                
                if len(s) <= 1:
                    return s + random_char
                
                if operation == 'delete':
                    return s[:-1]
                elif operation == 'substitute':
                    return s[:-1] + random_char
                else:  # insert
                    return s + random_char

            for i, col in enumerate(text_cols):
                col_mask = typo_mask[:, i]
                if col_mask.any():
                    # Apply the dynamic typo mutation function strictly to the masked cells
                    df.loc[col_mask, col] = df.loc[col_mask, col].apply(_mutate_string)

        return df



### Alternative Solutions

### Current Workaround / Alternative Code:

Currently, users and educators have to write verbose, loop-heavy, or non-vectorized custom functions to perturb their data cell-by-cell. A typical suboptimal workaround looks like this:

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import random

def manual_corrupt(df, num_nan=0, num_typos=0):
    df_dirty = df.copy()
    
    # 1. Non-vectorized manual loop for NaNs
    if num_nan > 0:
        for _ in range(num_nan):
            r = random.randint(0, df_dirty.shape[0] - 1)
            c = random.randint(0, df_dirty.shape[1] - 1)
            df_dirty.iat[r, c] = np.nan
            
    # 2. Slow cell-by-cell scalar string manipulation for typos
    text_cols = df_dirty.select_dtypes(include=['object', 'string']).columns
    if num_typos > 0 and len(text_cols) > 0:
        for _ in range(num_typos):
            r = random.randint(0, df_dirty.shape[0] - 1)
            col = random.choice(text_cols)
            current_val = str(df_dirty.at[r, col])
            # Naive typo injection (just drops the last character)
            if len(current_val) > 1:
                df_dirty.at[r, col] = current_val[:-1]
                
    return df_dirty


### Additional Context

This feature would significantly benefit the Data Science, Machine Learning, and AI educational ecosystem (such as university courses, bootcamps, and online interactive notebooks). 

Teaching "Data Cleaning" is notoriously hard without messy data, and creating realistic "dirty data" natively with one simple, reproducible function call (`df.corrupt(...)`) would seamlessly close the gap between pristine benchmark datasets and real-world preprocessing challenges. 

I am willing to contribute or iterate on this implementation based on core-dev feedback regarding where this best fits into the Pandas codebase.

flat_text_indices = np.random.choice(total_text_cells, size=n_typos, replace=False)
typo_mask = np.zeros(total_text_cells, dtype=bool).reshape(num_rows, len(text_cols))
typo_mask.ravel()[flat_text_indices] = True

def _mutate_string(val):
"""Helper to simulate realistic human typing errors dynamically."""
s = str(val)
if not s or s == "nan":
return s

# Randomly pick an error type: deletion, substitution, or insertion
operation = random.choice(['delete', 'substitute', 'insert'])
random_char = random.choice(string.ascii_lowercase)

if len(s) <= 1:
return s + random_char

if operation == 'delete':
return s[:-1]
elif operation == 'substitute':
return s[:-1] + random_char
else: # insert
return s + random_char

for i, col in enumerate(text_cols):
col_mask = typo_mask[:, i]
if col_mask.any():
# Apply the dynamic typo mutation function strictly to the masked cells
df.loc[col_mask, col] = df.loc[col_mask, col].apply(_mutate_string)

return df

Alternative Solutions

Current Workaround / Alternative Code:

Currently, users and educators have to write verbose, loop-heavy, or non-vectorized custom functions to perturb their data cell-by-cell. A typical suboptimal workaround looks like this:

df.corrupt(...)`) would seamlessly close the gap between pristine benchmark datasets and real-world preprocessing challenges.

I am willing to contribute or iterate on this implementation based on core-dev feedback regarding where this best fits into the Pandas codebase.

title: BUG: Timedeltas less than 1 second truncate to 0
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

print( pd.Timedelta( 1, 's' ) / 2, "Result should be '0 days 00:00:00.500000'" )
print( pd.Timedelta( 1, 'day' ) * 0.1111, "Result should be '0 days 02:39:59.040000'" )
print( pd.Timedelta( 1, 'ms' ) * 2, "This works in 3.0. Result should be '0 days 00:00:00.002000'")
print( pd.Timedelta( 1, 'ms' ) / 2, "Result should be '0 days 00:00:00.000500'")
```

### Issue Description

Timedeltas smaller than 1 second are truncated to 0 in some situations and larger Timedeltas are truncated to the nearest second. Similarly, dividing a 1 ms Timedelta by 2 is truncated to 0. This is distinct from the behavior reported in #65389, but they could be related. 

This bug occurs in 3.0.3 and 3.0.2, but not in pandas 2.2.

```
import pandas as pd

print( pd.Timedelta( 1, 's' ) / 2, "Result should be '0 days 00:00:00.500000'" )
print( pd.Timedelta( 1, 'day' ) * 0.1111, "Result should be '0 days 02:39:59.040000'" )
print( pd.Timedelta( 1, 'ms' ) * 2, "This works in 3.0. Result should be '0 days 00:00:00.002000'")
print( pd.Timedelta( 1, 'ms' ) / 2, "Result should be '0 days 00:00:00.000500'")
```

Thanks for all the work that you do to make pandas so excellent!

### Expected Behavior

The expected correct outputs are embedded in the code example. Dividing 1 second by 2 should give 0.5 second, not 0 second.

title: ENH: display.timedelta_format similar to display.float_format
body:
### Feature Type

- [x] Adding new functionality to pandas

- [ ] Changing existing functionality in pandas

- [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas


### Problem Description

I can set [`display.float_format`](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/options.html) to `"{:.1f}".format` to get a very short output, but `timedelta64[ns]` is still printed as `0 days 00:05:09.812000` instead of `5m` (as it would if I could set `display.timedelta_format` to my own function).


### Feature Description

Add `display.timedelta_format`, `display.timestamp_format` &c that can be set to a `callable` that takes an object of the specified type and returns a string.

### Alternative Solutions

Alternatively, replace `display.float_format` with a generic `display.format` which would receive an object (`int` or `float` or `Timestamp` or `Timedelta` or whatever) and return either a string or `NotImplemented` to indicate that the default (built-in) method is to be used.

Alternatively,  `display.format` can be a `dict` wher `display.format[float]` has the same meaning as `display.float_format` now.

### Additional Context

_No response_

title: API: GroupBy.agg() numeric_only deprecation with custom function
body:
Case: using `groupby().agg()` with a custom numpy funtion object (`np.mean`) instead of a built-in function string name (`"mean"`).

When using pandas 1.5, we get a future warning about numeric_only going to change (https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/46072), and that you can specify the keyword:

```python
>>> df = pd.DataFrame({"key": ['a', 'b', 'a'], "col1": ['a', 'b', 'c'], "col2": [1, 2, 3]})
>>> df
  key col1  col2
0   a    a     1
1   b    b     2
2   a    c     3
>>> df.groupby("key").agg(np.mean)
FutureWarning: The default value of numeric_only in DataFrameGroupBy.mean is deprecated. 
In a future version, numeric_only will default to False. Either specify numeric_only or select 
only columns which should be valid for the function.
     col2
key      
a     2.0
b     2.0

```

However, if you then try to specify the `numeric_only` keyword as suggested, you get an error:

```
In [10]: df.groupby("key").agg(np.mean, numeric_only=True)
...
File ~/miniconda3/envs/pandas15/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pandas/core/groupby/generic.py:981, in DataFrameGroupBy._aggregate_frame(self, func, *args, **kwargs)
    978 if self.axis == 0:
    979     # test_pass_args_kwargs_duplicate_columns gets here with non-unique columns
    980     for name, data in self.grouper.get_iterator(obj, self.axis):
--> 981         fres = func(data, *args, **kwargs)
    982         result[name] = fres
    983 else:
    984     # we get here in a number of test_multilevel tests

File <__array_function__ internals>:198, in mean(*args, **kwargs)

TypeError: mean() got an unexpected keyword argument 'numeric_only'
```

I know this is because when passing a custom function to `agg`, we pass through any kwargs, so `numeric_only` is also passed to `np.mean`, which of course doesn't know that keyword.

But this seems a bit confusing with the current warning message. So I am wondering if we should do at least either of both:

* If the current behaviour is intentional (`numeric_only` only working for built-in functions specified by string name), then the warning message could be updated to be clearer
* Could we actually support `numeric_only=True` for generic numpy functions / UDFs as well? (was this ever discussed before? Only searched briefly, but didn't directly find something)

cc @rhshadrach @jbrockmendel

title: PERF: pandas.io.sql.has_table slow for ADBC driver with large database
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this issue exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this issue exists on the main branch of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

We actually noticed the slowness from `pandas.read_sql`, but narrowed the problem down to its call to `has_table`. We're calling `read_sql` with a query so it will always be a miss for `has_table`.

```python
import time
import pandas.io.sql as pd_sql
from adbc_driver_snowflake.dbapi import connect

with connect("<database URI>") as conn:  # Connection to a large database
    start = time.perf_counter()
    pd_sql.has_table("foo", conn)  # Where foo does not match a table
    end = time.perf_counter()
    print(f"Took {end - start} seconds")
```

The problem sees to be in [`pandas.io.sql.ADBCDatabase.has_table`](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/main/pandas/io/sql.py#L2429-L2431), which calls [`self.con.adbc_get_objects()`](https://arrow.apache.org/adbc/current/python/api/adbc_driver_manager.html#adbc_driver_manager.dbapi.Connection.adbc_get_objects) without passing `depth=`. The default depth is `"all"`, which enumerates objects down to the columns. Adding `depth="tables"` speeds things up dramatically for us—from 53 seconds without `depth="tables"` down to 3 seconds with.

```python
import time
import pandas.io.sql as pd_sql
from adbc_driver_snowflake.dbapi import connect

with connect("<database URI>") as conn:  # Connection to a large database
    start = time.perf_counter()
    pd_sql.has_table("foo", conn)  # Where foo does not match a table
    end = time.perf_counter()
    print(f"Took {end - start} seconds")
```

The problem sees to be in [`pandas.io.sql.ADBCDatabase.has_table`](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/main/pandas/io/sql.py#L2429-L2431), which calls [`self.con.adbc_get_objects()`](https://arrow.apache.org/adbc/current/python/api/adbc_driver_manager.html#adbc_driver_manager.dbapi.Connection.adbc_get_objects) without passing `depth=`. The default depth is `"all"`, which enumerates objects down to the columns. Adding `depth="tables"` speeds things up dramatically for us—from 53 seconds without `depth="tables"` down to 3 seconds with.

title: Moving to PyArrow dtypes by default
body:
There have been some discussions about this before, but as far as I know there is not a plan or any decision made.

My understanding is (feel free to disagree):

1. If we were starting pandas today, we would **only** use Arrow as storage for DataFrame columns / Series
2. After all the great work that has been done on building new pandas types based on PyArrow, we are not considering other Arrow implementations
3. Based on 1 and 2, we are moving towards pandas based on PyArrow, and the main question is what's the transition path

@jbrockmendel [commented](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/61599#issuecomment-2957034897) this, and I think many others share this point of view, based on past interactions:
```
There's a path to making it feasible to use PyArrow types by default, but that path probably takes multiple major release cycles. Doing it for 3.0 would be, frankly, insane.
```

It would be interesting to know why exactly, I guess it's mainly because of two main reasons:
- Finish PyArrow types and making operations with them as reliable and fast as the original pandas types
- Giving users time to adapt

I don't know the exact state of the PyArrow types, and how often users will face problems if using them instead of the original ones. From my perception, there aren't any major efforts to make them better at this point. So, I'm unsure if the situation in that regard will be very different if we make the PyArrow types the default ones tomorrow, or if we make them the default ones in two years.

My understanding is that the only person who is paid consistently to work on pandas is Matt, and he's doing an amazing job at keeping the project going, reviewing most of the PRs, keeping the CI in good shape... But I don't think not him not anyone else if being able to put hours into developing new things as it used to be. For reference, this is the GitHub chart of pandas activity (commits) since pandas 2.0:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3c766972-49b1-4fff-ba21-ef52c71ade89)

So, in my opinion, the existing problems with PyArrow will start to be addressed significantly, whenever they become the default ones.

So, in my opinion our two main options are:

- We move forward with the PyArrow transition. pandas 3.0 will surely not be the best pandas version ever if we start using PyArrow types, but pandas 3.1 will be much better, and pandas 3.2 may be as good as pandas 2 in reliability and speed, but much closer to what we would like pandas to be.

Of course not all users are ready for pandas 3.0 with Arrow types. They can surely pin to `pandas=2` until pandas 3 is more mature and they made the required changes to their code. We can surely add a flag `pandas.options.mode.use_arrow = False` that reverts the new default to the old status quo. So users can actually move to pandas 3.0 but stay with the old types until we (pandas) and them are ready to get into the new default types. The transition from Python 2 to 3 (which is surely an example of what not to do) took more than 10 years. I don't think in our case we need as much. And if there is interest (aka money) we can also support the pandas 2 series while needed.

- The other option is to continue with the transition to the new nullable types, that my understanding is that we implemented because PyArrow didn't exist at that time. Continue to put our little resources on them. Making users adapt their code to a new temporary status quo, not the final one we envision, and stay in this transition period and delay the move to PyArrow I assume around 6 years (Brock mentioned `multiple major release cycles`, so I assume something like 3 at a rate of one major release every 2 years).

It will be great to know what are other people's thoughts and ideal plans, and see what makes more sense. But to me personally, based on the above information, it doesn't sound more insane to move to PyArrow in pandas 3, than to move in pandas 6.

title: Surprising type conversion when iterating
body:
#### Code Sample, a copy-pastable example if possible

```python
>>> df = pandas.DataFrame({'ints': range(5)})
>>> type(df.iloc[:, 0][0])
<class 'numpy.int64'>
>>> type(list(df.iloc[:, 0])[0])
<class 'int'>
```
#### Problem description

When iterating over a series, values are being converted to closest Python type and not left as an original type. This is problematic because it is a surprising change and leads to data manipulation one does not expect. One would hope that you can easily get what you stored into a dataframe out when iterating and not a variation of it.

The issue is that iterating uses internally numpy's [`tolist`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.14.0/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.tolist.html) which has this behavior. This is problematic for two reasons: it constructs the whole list first in memory (#20783) and converts to "closest Python type".

#### Expected Output

```python
>>> df = pandas.DataFrame({'ints': range(5)})
>>> type(df.iloc[:, 0][0])
<class 'numpy.int64'>
>>> type(list(df.iloc[:, 0])[0])
<class 'numpy.int64'>
```

#### Output of ``pd.show_versions()``

title: ENH: Methods to potentially change for future.python_scalars
body:
The method lists below were generated by #64264 which might have some gaps but should be fairly exhaustive of the public API. I've grouped them into how I think they should be handled, but this will certainly need consensus.

Should be changed
- DataFrame.kurt
- DataFrame.max
- DataFrame.mean
- DataFrame.median
- DataFrame.min
- DataFrame.skew
- Index.all
- Index.any
- NDFrame.squeeze
- NDFrame.xs
- Series.searchsorted
- RangeIndex.searchsorted
- NDFrame.asof
- NDFrame.get
- Index.equals
- Index.get_slice_bound
- Index.identical
- CategoricalIndex.searchsorted
- IndexOpsMixin.searchsorted
- `Series.__getitem__`

Unclear
- Index.argmax
- Index.argmin
- Index.max
- Index.min
- IndexOpsMixin.argmax
- IndexOpsMixin.argmin
- IndexOpsMixin.item
- NDFrame.first_valid_index
- NDFrame.last_valid_index
- Series.idxmax
- Series.idxmin
- DataFrame.eval (`df.eval("True > 6")`)

Should not be changed
- _NaT.to_datetime64
- _NaT.to_numpy
- _Timedelta.to_numpy
- _Timedelta.to_timedelta64
- _Timestamp.to_datetime64
- _Timestamp.to_numpy

title: ENH: Uniformised the boolean results in Pandas
body:
Hello everybody,

I'm not completely sure this issue has not been submitted earlier, please accept my apologies if so. It is also possible that what I saw results from an incorrect use of the Pandas package.

It seems that boolean answers are not always rigourosly the same in Pandas, which is really disturbing, and can involve errors when developping scripts using Pandas.

Here are examples of the problem (realized with Python 3.8.5 and Pandas 1.0.5). Let's imagine we have a DataFrame named "data", with a column "col1". We are first using the all() function:

>>>data["col1"].all()
Out: True
>>>data["col1"].all() == True
Out: True
>>>data["col1"].all() is True
False

In this case, the checking of the value using the PEP recommended "is" return a wrong answer.

Same idea with the "empty" function :

>>>data["col1].empty
Out: False
>>>data["col1"].empty == False
Out: True
>>>data["col1"].empty is False
Out: True

This time, checking the value using the "is" statement works well.

From what I understand, some boolean answers are of different "types" (Numpy logical booleans, ...).

Maybe a uniformisation of the boolean answer would avoid these kinds of problems and could be an improvment for future Pandas versions ?
