text:
LinearSVC crammer_singer reports a garbage n_iter_

> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

For `LinearSVC(multi_class="crammer_singer")` with three or more classes,
`clf.n_iter_` does not report the number of iterations the Crammer–Singer solver
actually ran. It reports a large, run-to-run unstable integer drawn from
out-of-bounds reads of the LIBLINEAR heap. As a downstream consequence,
`_fit_liblinear` then compares this garbage value against `max_iter` and emits
`ConvergenceWarning` on essentially every fit — including fits where the solver
converged in a few thousand epochs and `max_iter` is set to `10_000_000`.


**User-facing impact:**

- `clf.n_iter_` is not a usable signal for CS. It changes between Python
  processes (ASLR re-rolls the heap layout on each `execve`), so any code that
  inspects it — hyper-parameter search summaries, learning-curve diagnostics,
  user-written convergence checks — sees noise.
- Because the garbage almost always exceeds `max_iter`, `ConvergenceWarning`
  fires on every CS fit with K ≥ 3, even when the solver has converged. The
  recommended remedy (raising `max_iter`) doesn't help.


- Every a few times even `10_000_000` max_iter raises convergence warning.


### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
import warnings
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
from sklearn.svm import LinearSVC
from sklearn.exceptions import ConvergenceWarning

X, y = load_digits(return_X_y=True)   # K=10 classes; any K>=3 triggers the bug

with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as caught:
    warnings.simplefilter("always", ConvergenceWarning)
    clf = LinearSVC(
        multi_class="crammer_singer",
        max_iter=10_000_000,   # absurdly large — bug still fires
        random_state=0,
        dual=True,
    ).fit(X, y)
    warn_fired = any(
        issubclass(w.category, ConvergenceWarning) for w in caught
    )

print("clf.n_iter_      :", clf.n_iter_)
print("ConvergenceWarn? :", warn_fired)
```
On my machine it fires 3 out of 10 times which is obviously absurd.


**Mechanism — three cooperating defects:**

1. **Allocation site** (`sklearn/svm/src/liblinear/linear.cpp`): `train()`
   allocates exactly **one** `int` for `model_->n_iter` in the `MCSVM_CS` branch
   and writes only slot `[0]`:
   ```cpp
   model_->n_iter = Malloc(int, 1);
   // ...
   model_->n_iter[0] = Solver.Solve(model_->w);
   ```

2. **Out-of-bounds read** : `get_n_iter()` copies
   `nr_class` slots into the caller's buffer. There is a special case for
   `nr_class == 2` (the binary path, which also allocates one slot), but **no**
   case for `MCSVM_CS`:
   ```cpp
   void get_n_iter(const model *model_, int* n_iter)
   {
       int labels = model_->nr_class;
       if (labels == 2)      // binary special-cased correctly...
           labels = 1;
                             // ...but MCSVM_CS with K>=3 still loops K times
       if (model_->n_iter != NULL)
           for (int i = 0; i < labels; i++)
               n_iter[i] = model_->n_iter[i];   // slots [1..K-1] are OOB reads
   }
   ```
   
3. **Warning fired off the wrong value** (`sklearn/svm/_base.py`):
   ```python
   n_iter_max = max(n_iter_)   # dominated by heap garbage in slots [1..K-1]
   if n_iter_max >= max_iter:
       warnings.warn("Liblinear failed to converge, ...")
   ```
   `LinearSVC._classes.py` then stores `self.n_iter_ = n_iter_.max().item()`,
   exposing the same garbage value to the user as a scalar `int`.



### Expected Results


`clf.n_iter_` is a small positive integer reflecting the number of CS epochs
actually run (a few thousand on this dataset), and `ConvergenceWarning` is not
raised because the solver converged well within `max_iter`.

### Actual Results

`clf.n_iter_` is a large integer that has no relation to the number of epochs the
solver ran, and changes between Python processes. Example output on Linux x86_64
/ glibc:

```
clf.n_iter_      : 856261606
ConvergenceWarn? : True
```

The warning text is the standard one:

```
ConvergenceWarning: Liblinear failed to converge, increase the number of iterations.
```

The internal `n_iter_` buffer (the numpy array `_fit_liblinear` returns before
`LinearSVC.fit` collapses it to a scalar via `.max().item()`) shows what is
actually happening:

```
slot 0 (real epoch count) : 8401       ← solver converged; well below max_iter=10_000_000
slots 1..9 (garbage)      : [25114, 0, 0, -244463840, 232920324, 1249, 0, 2, 0]
max(n_iter_)              : 232920324  ← heap contents reinterpreted as int32; this is what becomes clf.n_iter_
```



### Versions

title: RFC/META Harden CI security
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

Because of the ongoing Mini Shai Hulud attack against CI & OSS supply chain (see [Postmortem: TanStack npm supply-chain compromise](https://tanstack.com/blog/npm-supply-chain-compromise-postmortem) for details), I think we should review and possibly straighten our CI before releasing 1.9.

In particular, we use the unsafe `pull_request_target` in several workflows which is a documented attack vector of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm that currently still spreads by corrupting an increasing number of npm and pypi packages.

## Short term:

- Regularly check that the dates of https://pypi.org/project/scikit-learn/#files match our manually triggered release dates (this seems to be the case as of today 2026-05-13)
- Review our `publish_pypi.yml` and `wheels.yml` workflows to make sure it is not subject by cache poisoning attacks (from a cursory glance it does not think we use any build cache, but we should double-check).
- Review findings reported by `zizmor .github/workflows/publish_pypi.yml` and `zizmor .github/workflows/wheels.yml`.
- Assess if workflows that use `pull_request_target` can have a side effect on our release process.
  - Note: I asked the following to Claude: https://claude.ai/share/c6aea06a-a9dc-4e55-b5f4-830254166994 (TL;DNR it isn't too worried, but we should probably spend more time reading this analysis in more details).
- Unzip and review the contents of the generated wheel files in the release branch prior to uploading to look for unexpected payload.
  - EDIT: I asked Claude (same conversation as the one linked above) to do that on the latest nightly wheel and it did not find anything fishy.

## Medium/Long term

- Hash-pin all github actions used in the `publish_pypi.yml` and `wheels.yml` workflows.
- Use relative dependency cooldown for GitHub Actions updates via dependabot:
  - https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/reference/supply-chain-security/dependabot-options-reference?versionId=free-pro-team%40latest&productId=code-security&restPage=tutorials%2Csecure-your-dependencies%2Cautomating-dependabot-with-github-actions#cooldown-
- Find ways to avoid the use of `pull_request_target` in our regular dev CI.
- Address any remaining "findings" reported by running `zizmor .` in our repo (currently `191 findings (73 suppressed, 32 fixable): 4 informational, 26 low, 1 medium, 87 high`, many of which are related to `pull_request_target` and unpinned action references).
- Use relative dependency cooldowns when generating lock files as soon as implemented in mamba and conda-lock:
  - https://github.com/conda/conda-lock/issues/908
  - https://github.com/mamba-org/mamba/pull/4228
- Isolate the release process in a dedicated repo with a easy to audit, limited number of config files/action logs/user permissions, see:
  - https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/29178
  - https://github.com/numpy/numpy-release
  - https://github.com/scipy/scipy-release


I labeled this PR as block as I think we should at least do the short terms items prior to releasing.

We can probably create sub-issues for things we agree are good ideas.

title: Using dask distributed with a pipeline and column_transformer breaks metadata routing

### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

when using a pipeline with a columns transformer, using the pipeline in a distributed environment seems to raise a strange error.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.compose import make_column_transformer
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, QuantileTransformer
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

X, y = make_classification()

pipeline = make_pipeline(
    make_column_transformer(
        (StandardScaler(), [0]),
    ),
    # StandardScaler(),
    RandomForestClassifier(
        max_depth=7, n_estimators=300, random_state=42
    )
)
pipeline

cv_results = cross_val_score(pipeline, X, y, cv=5, n_jobs=1)
print("CV score:", np.mean(cv_results))

from distributed import Client
from joblib import parallel_config

client = Client()

with parallel_config("dask"):
    cv_scores = cross_val_score(pipeline, X, y, cv=5, n_jobs=-1)

print("CV score:", np.mean(cv_scores))
client.close()
```

### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

when using a pipeline with a columns transformer, using the pipeline in a distributed environment seems to raise a strange error.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.compose import make_column_transformer
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, QuantileTransformer
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

X, y = make_classification()

pipeline = make_pipeline(
    make_column_transformer(
        (StandardScaler(), [0]),
    ),
    # StandardScaler(),
    RandomForestClassifier(
        max_depth=7, n_estimators=300, random_state=42
    )
)
pipeline

cv_results = cross_val_score(pipeline, X, y, cv=5, n_jobs=1)
print("CV score:", np.mean(cv_results))

from distributed import Client
from joblib import parallel_config

client = Client()

with parallel_config("dask"):
    cv_scores = cross_val_score(pipeline, X, y, cv=5, n_jobs=-1)

print("CV score:", np.mean(cv_scores))
client.close()
```

title: Array API: Ridge with alpha as an array fails due to misconfigured checks
body:
### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

When trying to fit a ridge model with the array API on multi target objective, if one want to pass an array for alpha, the checks are not well configured I think, leading to an impossibility.
Here is a small reproducer.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
import os
os.environ["SCIPY_ARRAY_API"] = "1"

import torch

from sklearn import config_context
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.datasets import make_regression

X, y = make_regression(1000, n_targets=10)
with config_context(array_api_dispatch=True):
    # will consider that alpha is scalar in `_ridge_regression`
    # and fail the check_scalar check l.703
    reg = Ridge(alpha=np.array([1.]*10))
    reg.fit(torch.tensor(X), torch.tensor(y))

     # Will fail on `_validate_params` from the base estimator class
    reg = Ridge(alpha=torch.tensor([1.]*10))
    reg.fit(torch.tensor(X), torch.tensor(y))
```

### Expected Results

Working with both cases :)

### Actual Results

raise errors:

- for np.array

If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the workflow you want to enable

I'm not sure if this has already been requested, but i haven't seen any discussion about this yet.

The `_parameter_constraint` attribute works for hyperparameter validation, but it can easily adapted to make hyperparameters available, and their ranges programatically accesible.

This would be great for any AutoML application that deals with hyperparameter optimization, since there would be an officially supported interface within which you can build the search space in which to optimize. To avoid making too many changes, we could expect this kind of workflows to filter parameters like `n_jobs`, `copy_X` or other internal parameters instead of making the distinction a the package level.  

### Describe your proposed solution

I see 2 possible ways to implement this:
1. Rename all `_parameter_constraint` attributes to `parameter_constraint`, making it clear that it is publicly accesible, and add references to it across the documentation pages.
2. Provide a new public method that returns the values in `_parameter_constaints`, adding a reference to it in the various documentation pages.

### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

Users can already access `_parameter_constraints`. The only issue is that it's a private method and not well documented (which i understand, since now it's only meant for internal parameter validation).

### Additional context

_No response_

title: r2_score() no longer supports `pyarrow.ChunkedArray` inputs
body:
### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

In `scikit-learn` 1.8.0,`r2_score()` and other metric functions supported `pyarrow.ChunkedArray` input.

With the latest nightlies, they raise a hard-to-understand error from inside `narwhals`.

I suspect this was broken by #31127. Reporting because this showed up in `lightgbm`'s tests using the latest `scikit-learn` nightlies (https://github.com/lightgbm-org/LightGBM/issues/7269).

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

Using Python 3.14 just as an example, I don't have any reason to suspect this is sensitive to Python version.

```shell
docker run \
  --rm \
  -it python:3.14 \
  bash

pip install \
  --prefer-binary \
  --extra-index-url https://pypi.anaconda.org/scientific-python-nightly-wheels/simple \
  'cffi>=2.0.0' \
  'numpy>=2.5.0.dev0' \
  'pandas>=3.1.0.dev0' \
  'pyarrow>=24.0.0.dev0' \
  'scikit-learn>=1.9.dev0' \
  'scipy>=1.18.0.dev0'

cat > test.py <<EOF
import pandas as pd
from pyarrow import chunked_array
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score

y_true = pd.Series([0.1, 0.2, 0.3])
y_pred = chunked_array(y_true)
print(f"R2: {r2_score(y_true, y_pred)}")
EOF

# this fails (see "Actual Results")
python ./test.py
```

Installing the latest `scikit-learn` release (1.8.0) into the environment and leaving everything else the same, the example passes.

```console
$ pip install --no-deps 'scikit-learn==1.8.0'
$ python ./test.py
R2: 1.0
```

### Expected Results

`r2_score()` (and other metric functions) to accept `pyarrow.ChunkedArray` inputs and process them correctly.

### Actual Results

```text
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "//./test.py", line 7, in <module>
    r2_score(y_true, y_pred)
    ~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.14/site-packages/sklearn/utils/_param_validation.py", line 218, in wrapper
    return func(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.14/site-packages/sklearn/metrics/_regression.py", line 1288, in r2_score
    _check_reg_targets_with_floating_dtype(
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^
        y_true, y_pred, sample_weight, multioutput, xp=xp
        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
    )
    ^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.14/site-packages/sklearn/metrics/_regression.py", line 209, in _check_reg_targets_with_floating_dtype
    y_type, y_true, y_pred, sample_weight, multioutput = _check_reg_targets(
                                                         ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^
        y_true, y_pred, sample_weight, multioutput, dtype=dtype_name, xp=xp
        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
    )
    ^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.14/site-packages/sklearn/metrics/_regression.py", line 114, in _check_reg_targets
    check_consistent_length(y_true, y_pred, sample_weight)
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.14/site-packages/sklearn/utils/validation.py", line 453, in check_consistent_length
    lengths = [_num_samples(X) for X in arrays if X is not None]
               ~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.14/site-packages/sklearn/utils/validation.py", line 381, in _num_samples
    return nw.from_native(x).shape[0]
           ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.14/site-packages/narwhals/stable/v2/__init__.py", line 484, in from_native
    return _from_native_impl(  # type: ignore[no-any-return]
        native_object,
    ...<5 lines>...
        version=Version.V2,
    )
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.14/site-packages/narwhals/translate.py", line 383, in _from_native_impl
    raise TypeError(msg)
TypeError: Please set `allow_series=True` or `series_only=True`
```

### Versions

<details><summary>output of 'pip list' (click me)</summary>

```console
$ pip list
Package         Version
--------------- --------------------------
cffi            2.0.0
joblib          1.5.3
narwhals        2.21.0
numpy           2.5.0.dev0
pandas          3.1.0.dev0+893.g9187363089
pip             26.1.1
pyarrow         25.0.0.dev48
pycparser       3.0
python-dateutil 2.9.0.post0
scikit-learn    1.9.dev0
scipy           1.18.0.dev0
six             1.17.0
threadpoolctl   3.6.0
```

</details>

### Interest in fixing the bug

Not available to put up a PR in the near future, sorry.

title: Use automatic chunk size in `ledoit_wolf_shrinkage` (and friends?)
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

This issue is a tracker for the task of adding chunking to the computation in `ledoit_wolf_shrinkage` (`sklearn/covariance/_shrunk_covariance.py`).

For the CPU implementation chunking is already supported. For the array API path we currently perform no chunking. Bigger chunks are better for GPU devices (in terms of execution time), but they require more memory.

After investigating and benchmarking a bit, I am not sure what we should do. For "tall and skinny" datasets blocking doesn't matter. For wide datasets it limits how much additional memory is used for temporaries. With `n_features=20_000` you'd use something like 1.6GB of extra memory without blocking. However, the speedup (MPS over numpy) when using blocking is much smaller than if the MPS implementation runs without blocking. It can be  5x - 10x without blocking and only something like 2x faster if you use `block_size=1000` for MPS as well.

Numpy is also faster if you increase the block size. For example, `block_size=2000` seems to speed things up by roughly a factor two and still only uses a small amount of memory.

So I think we could increase the block size, even for Numpy. But I am not sure how we should decide the trade-off between speed up and memory. We could use a block size like 3000 for all namespaces/devices to get most of the speed up and avoid catastrophic "out of memory" exceptions. The user could then tune the block size if they knew that they have enough memory? But it feels like I just made up 3000, it could equally be 4000 or 2000 - I couldn't argue which of these is more correct/better.

Quoting @ogrisel's comment here to make it easy to find:

> I suspect that for CPU devices (e.g. NumPy), the optimal block size should be set that the sum of intermediate arrays stays in Level 3 CPU cache (typically a few megabytes on most modern CPUs).
>
> It should be possible to tell if a given device is CPU or not using something like `getattr(array, "__dlpack_device__", lambda: (1, 0))()[0] in (1, 3)`.

A suggestion is to switch to using `block_size="auto"` as the default (instead of 1000). This would allow us to use a different value for different devices (eg CPUs vs GPUs). Maybe we could even determine the "best" size based on the L3 cache size, but maybe this is making things too complicated.

xref https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/33573/changes#r3028299959

title: Improve `pairwise_distances` so that it works with array API inputs for all supported metrics
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

The `pairwise_distances` function can't always be called when using array API. We should fix this.

`pairwise_distances` with `metric="mahalanobis"` (and other scipy-delegated metrics not in `PAIRWISE_DISTANCE_FUNCTIONS`) does not work with array API inputs on non-CPU devices.

The `"mahalanobis"` metric is not in scikit-learn's `PAIRWISE_DISTANCE_FUNCTIONS`, so `pairwise_distances` falls through to the scipy fallback path, which calls `scipy.spatial.distance.cdist` / `scipy.spatial.distance.pdist`. As of scipy 1.17.1, both functions call `np.asarray()` on their inputs, which fails for GPU tensors (e.g. PyTorch MPS/CUDA, CuPy).

I'm not sure if the solution for this is to implement this in scikit-learn or wait for scipy to add support for it. For other metrics we have implementations in Python in `sklearn/metrics/pairwise.py`, so maybe there is no performance downside to having a Python implementation? If yes, then this is "just" a question of turning the handle to implement new distances?
 
xref https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/33573/changes#r3079594482

title: CountVectorizer fails when dealing with short strings and ngram_range > len of the strings
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

I was debugging a problem with the skrub GapEncoder in https://github.com/skrub-data/skrub/issues/770, and I realized that the root of the problem is somewhere in the CountVectorizer, specifically when it receives a column that includes only strings of length 1 (`"A"`). 

After tracking down the problem, my understanding is that the CountVectorizer is failing because all strings in the column are shorter than the minimum value in `ngram_range` (see the example). 

The result is the exception
```
ValueError: empty vocabulary; perhaps the documents only contain stop words
```

which is not giving me a lot of information to debug. I think the error message could be improved so that it describes the situation in more detail. 

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
import string

rng = np.random.default_rng()
letters = rng.choice(list(string.ascii_letters), 1000)

# works
CountVectorizer(analyzer="char", ).fit_transform(np.array(letters))
# raises ValueError: empty vocabulary; perhaps the documents only contain stop words
CountVectorizer(analyzer="char", ngram_range=(2,4)).fit_transform(np.array(letters))
```

### Expected Results

I think it makes sense that the exception is raised, so the current behavior is "expected". I would rather have a more informative error if possible, however. Something like "the vocabulary is empty because all values in the series are shorter than the minimum value of ngram_range". 

### Actual Results

```
ValueError: empty vocabulary; perhaps the documents only contain stop words
```

### Versions

```shell
System:
    python: 3.13.12 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Feb  5 2026, 06:11:05) [Clang 19.1.7 ]
executable: /Users/rcap/work/skrub/.pixi/envs/dev/bin/python
   machine: macOS-15.7.4-arm64-arm-64bit-Mach-O

Python dependencies:
      sklearn: 1.8.0
          pip: None
   setuptools: 82.0.1
        numpy: 2.4.3
        scipy: 1.17.1
       Cython: None
       pandas: 3.0.2
   matplotlib: 3.10.8
       joblib: 1.5.3
threadpoolctl: 3.6.0

Built with OpenMP: True

threadpoolctl info:
       user_api: blas
   internal_api: openblas
    num_threads: 10
         prefix: libopenblas
       filepath: /Users/rcap/work/skrub/.pixi/envs/dev/lib/libopenblas.0.dylib
        version: 0.3.32
threading_layer: openmp
   architecture: VORTEX

       user_api: openmp
   internal_api: openmp
    num_threads: 10
         prefix: libomp
       filepath: /Users/rcap/work/skrub/.pixi/envs/dev/lib/libomp.dylib
        version: None
```

### Interest in fixing the bug

I'd rather leave this PR to someone else

title: Native categorical splitting enhancements in DecisionTree, ExtraTree, RandomForest and ExtraTrees
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the workflow you want to enable

From: https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/33354#issue-3973551384, we want to enable:

1. [ ] [Feature] Add a bitset cache (i.e. a uint64_t memory view) that allows us to compute the category split as we traverse the tree for inference speedup
2. [ ] [Feature] Brute-force search; expands categorical support to multi-class and multi-output problems
3. [ ] [Refactor] Consolidate the goes_left operation to work at inference time too
4. [x] [Refactor] Understand the bitset operations and merge these with the HistGradientBoosting bitset operations
5. [ ] [Feature] Implement categorical splitting for RandomSplitter for ExtraTrees. Pretty easy now that we have an arbitrary sized bitset, which we can expand to hold many categories in the case of a randomized tree
6. [ ] [Feature] Add support for sparse categorical splitting with best and random splitter
7. [ ] [Feature] Add support for categorical in exporting 
8. [ ] [Feature] Add internal ordinal encoding support for any kind of "categorical input" (see https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/33354#discussion_r3199995969)

### Describe your proposed solution

These work streams may not all be relevant, or mergable, but we can explore.

title: [FEA] Add Ambiguity Range metric for safety-critical classification
body: > [!WARNING] > This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label. ### Describe the workflow you want to enable Practitioners in safety-critical fields—such as medical diagnostics, structural engineering, or high-stakes financial modeling—require a method to explicitly quantify the "Indecision Zone" of a binary classifier. While aggregate metrics like the F1-score or Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) provide a high-level view of model performance, they often mask the specific regions where a model's prediction is statistically equivalent to guessing. By implementing the ambiguity_range_score, we enable a Tri-Modal Decision Workflow: Automated Action (High Confidence): Samples with scores above the Upper Threshold ($T_{upper}$, representing 100% Precision) or below the Lower Threshold ($T_{lower}$, representing 100% Recall) are handled automatically by the system with high trust. Human-in-the-Loop (Ambiguity Zone): Samples falling within the range $\mathcal{A} = T_{upper} - T_{lower}$ are flagged for manual expert review. Reliability Benchmarking: This allows developers to select models that minimize required human intervention by reducing the Ambiguity Range, even in cases where traditional metrics like AUC remain static. Safety-Critical Calibration: Practitioners can visualize exactly how much of a population falls into a "Gray Zone" where the model cannot guarantee a certain outcome. ### Describe your proposed solution I propose adding a new metric: ambiguity_range_score(y_true, y_score, *, epsilon=0.0, pos_label=None). Key Technical Implementations: The Ambiguity Measure ($\mathcal{A}$): Defined as the difference between the threshold required for perfect precision and the threshold required for perfect recall. Epsilon ($\epsilon$) Robustness: To handle real-world datasets containing outliers or label noise, the epsilon parameter allows for a "Soft Ambiguity" measure (e.g., $\epsilon=0.01$ for 99% precision/recall). This prevents a single mislabeled data point from artificially maximizing the range to 1.0. Integration: The function utilizes the existing precision_recall_curve logic to find the critical boundary points, ensuring consistency with scikit-learn’s internal API. ### Threshold Independence & Model Robustness A common limitation of standard classification metrics is their dependence on an arbitrary decision threshold (e.g., 0.5). Choosing a single threshold is often a heuristic choice made during deployment that may not reflect the inherent separability of the model. The ambiguity_range_score is superior in this regard because it is threshold-independent. Rather than evaluating performance at a single point, it quantifies the "Indecision Zone" as a global characteristic of the model's feature-space quality. By measuring the distance between the two extreme bounds of reliability ($T_{upper}$ and $T_{lower}$), it provides a scalar value that describes model "resoluteness" regardless of where an individual user might eventually set their operating point. ### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant I have considered using the Detection Error Tradeoff (DET) curve; however, while DET curves are excellent for visual analysis, they do not provide a direct, scalar "range" value that can be easily used for automated model selection or as a clear KPI for "Human-in-the-Loop" resource planning. ### Additional context I have attached a figure that provides an empirical verification of the metric: Distribution Density Plot: Illustrates the score overlapping that generates the Ambiguity Range ($\mathcal{A}$) and the resulting Ternary Decision Logic. Sensitivity Analysis: Demonstrates how the metric responds to increasing Label Noise vs. Feature Noise, and confirms that the epsilon parameter successfully maintains metric robustness in noisy environments. Developed and proposed by Kian Maleki. Here is the proposed implementation for `ambiguity_range_score`: ```python import numpy as np from sklearn.utils import check_consistent_length from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve def ambiguity_range_score(y_true, y_score, *, epsilon=0.0, pos_label=None): """Compute the Ambiguity Measure between perfect precision and recall. The Ambiguity Measure is the difference between the threshold required for perfect precision and the threshold required for perfect recall. Parameters ---------- y_true : array-like of shape (n_samples,) True binary labels. y_score : array-like of shape (n_samples,) Target scores, can either be probability estimates of the positive class or confidence values. epsilon : float, default=0.0 The tolerance for "perfect" metrics. (e.g., 0.01 for 99% precision/recall). pos_label : int, float, bool or str, default=None The label of the positive class. Returns ------- ambiguity : float The calculated range between thresholds. A smaller value indicates better class separability. """ check_consistent_length(y_true, y_score) # Calculate Precision-Recall curve and thresholds precision, recall, thresholds = precision_recall_curve( y_true, y_score, pos_label=pos_label ) # 1. Upper Threshold: Minimum score where Precision >= 1 - epsilon target_p = 1.0 - epsilon p_indices = np.where(precision >= target_p)[0] # Clip index for thresholds t_upper = thresholds[p_indices[0]] if p_indices.size > 0 else np.max(y_score) # 2. Lower Threshold: Maximum score where Recall >= 1 - epsilon target_r = 1.0 - epsilon r_indices = np.where(recall >= target_r)[0] t_lower = thresholds[r_indices[-1]] if r_indices.size > 0 else np.min(y_score) # 3. Ambiguity Measure return float(t_upper - t_lower) # Example Usage: if __name__ == "__main__": y_true = np.array([0, 0, 1, 1]) y_scores = np.array([0.1, 0.4, 0.35, 0.8]) print(f"Ambiguity Measure: {ambiguity_range_score(y_true, y_scores)}")

By implementing the ambiguity_range_score, we enable a Tri-Modal Decision Workflow:

Automated Action (High Confidence): Samples with scores above the Upper Threshold ($T_{upper}$, representing 100% Precision) or below the Lower Threshold ($T_{lower}$, representing 100% Recall) are handled automatically by the system with high trust.

Human-in-the-Loop (Ambiguity Zone): Samples falling within the range $\mathcal{A} = T_{upper} - T_{lower}$ are flagged for manual expert review.

Reliability Benchmarking: This allows developers to select models that minimize required human intervention by reducing the Ambiguity Range, even in cases where traditional metrics like AUC remain static.

Safety-Critical Calibration: Practitioners can visualize exactly how much of a population falls into a "Gray Zone" where the model cannot guarantee a certain outcome.

Describe your proposed solution

I propose adding a new metric: ambiguity_range_score(y_true, y_score, *, epsilon=0.0, pos_label=None).

Key Technical Implementations:
The Ambiguity Measure ($\mathcal{A}$): Defined as the difference between the threshold required for perfect precision and the threshold required for perfect recall.

Epsilon ($\epsilon$) Robustness: To handle real-world datasets containing outliers or label noise, the epsilon parameter allows for a "Soft Ambiguity" measure (e.g., $\epsilon=0.01$ for 99% precision/recall). This prevents a single mislabeled data point from artificially maximizing the range to 1.0.

Integration: The function utilizes the existing precision_recall_curve logic to find the critical boundary points, ensuring consistency with scikit-learn’s internal API.

Threshold Independence & Model Robustness

A common limitation of standard classification metrics is their dependence on an arbitrary decision threshold (e.g., 0.5). Choosing a single threshold is often a heuristic choice made during deployment that may not reflect the inherent separability of the model.

The ambiguity_range_score is superior in this regard because it is threshold-independent. Rather than evaluating performance at a single point, it quantifies the "Indecision Zone" as a global characteristic of the model's feature-space quality. By measuring the distance between the two extreme bounds of reliability ($T_{upper}$ and $T_{lower}$), it provides a scalar value that describes model "resoluteness" regardless of where an individual user might eventually set their operating point.

Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

I have considered using the Detection Error Tradeoff (DET) curve; however, while DET curves are excellent for visual analysis, they do not provide a direct, scalar "range" value that can be easily used for automated model selection or as a clear KPI for "Human-in-the-Loop" resource planning.

Additional context

I have attached a figure that provides an empirical verification of the metric:

Distribution Density Plot: Illustrates the score overlapping that generates the Ambiguity Range ($\mathcal{A}$) and the resulting Ternary Decision Logic.

Sensitivity Analysis: Demonstrates how the metric responds to increasing Label Noise vs. Feature Noise, and confirms that the epsilon parameter successfully maintains metric robustness in noisy environments.

Developed and proposed by Kian Maleki.

Here is the proposed implementation for :

`python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.utils import check_consistent_length
from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve

def ambiguity_range_score(y_true, y_score, *, epsilon=0.0, pos_label=None):
"""Compute the Ambiguity Measure between perfect precision and recall.

The Ambiguity Measure is the difference between the threshold required
for perfect precision and the threshold required for perfect recall.

Parameters
----------
y_true : array-like of shape (n_samples,)
True binary labels.
y_score : array-like of shape (n_samples,)
Target scores, can either be probability estimates of the positive
class or confidence values.
epsilon : float, default=0.0
The tolerance for "perfect" metrics. (e.g., 0.01 for 99% precision/recall).
pos_label : int, float, bool or str, default=None
The label of the positive class.

Returns
-------
ambiguity : float
The calculated range between thresholds. A smaller value indicates
better class separability.
"""
check_consistent_length(y_true, y_score)

# Calculate Precision-Recall curve and thresholds
precision, recall, thresholds = precision_recall_curve(
y_true, y_score, pos_label=pos_label
)

# 1. Upper Threshold: Minimum score where Precision >= 1 - epsilon
target_p = 1.0 - epsilon
p_indices = np.where(precision >= target_p)[0]

# Clip index for thresholds
t_upper = thresholds[p_indices[0]] if p_indices.size > 0 else np.max(y_score)

# 2. Lower Threshold: Maximum score where Recall >= 1 - epsilon
target_r = 1.0 - epsilon
r_indices = np.where(recall >= target_r)[0]

t_lower = thresholds[r_indices[-1]] if r_indices.size > 0 else np.min(y_score)

# 3. Ambiguity Measure
return float(t_upper - t_lower)

Example Usage:
if name == " main ":
y_true = np.array([0, 0, 1, 1])
y_scores = np.array([0.1, 0.4, 0.35, 0.8])
print(f"Ambiguity Measure: {ambiguity_range_score(y_true, y_scores)}")

title: PERF: Avoid unnecessary n_samples x n_samples diagonal sparse matrix in `_rescale_data`
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the workflow you want to enable

When fitting any linear model (e.g. `LinearRegression`, `Ridge`, `ElasticNet`) on sparse data
with `sample_weight`, the internal function `_rescale_data` is called to bake sample weights
into the data before solving. Currently it constructs an intermediate `(n_samples x n_samples)`
diagonal sparse matrix just to perform a simple row scaling, then runs a full sparse matrix
multiply through `safe_sparse_dot`.

For large sparse datasets this creates unnecessary memory allocation and computation on every
`fit()` call. The issue affects all linear models that support `sample_weight` with sparse input.

### Describe your proposed solution

Replace the `dia_array` + `safe_sparse_dot` approach with a direct elementwise row-scaling via
`csr_array.multiply()`, which is built for this exact operation and needs no intermediate matrix.

**Current code** in `sklearn/linear_model/_base.py:252`:

```python
if sp.issparse(X) or sp.issparse(y):
    sw_matrix = sparse.dia_array(
        (sample_weight_sqrt, 0), shape=(n_samples, n_samples)
    )

if sp.issparse(X):
    X = safe_sparse_dot(sw_matrix, X)

if sp.issparse(y):
    y = safe_sparse_dot(sw_matrix, y)
```

**Proposed code:**

```python
if sp.issparse(X):
    X = X.multiply(sample_weight_sqrt[:, None])

if sp.issparse(y):
    sw = sample_weight_sqrt[:, None] if y.ndim > 1 else sample_weight_sqrt
    y = y.multiply(sw)
```

Both produce identical results — verified against the existing `test_rescale_data` test and
directly against the old implementation across all input combinations.

**Why it is better:**

- Eliminates the `(n_samples x n_samples)` diagonal sparse matrix allocation entirely
- `csr_array.multiply(vector)` is O(nnz) with no intermediate object;
  `dia_array @ csr_array` constructs the diagonal object first, then runs a matrix-multiply
  code path
- For 100k samples: old code allocates a 100k-element `data` array and shape tuple for the
  `dia_array` before touching `X`; new code works directly on the `nnz` non-zero entries

## Describe alternatives you've considered

- `scipy.sparse.diags(sample_weight_sqrt) @ X` — same problem, still builds an intermediate
  diagonal sparse matrix
- Keeping `safe_sparse_dot` — unnecessary here since `.multiply()` handles elementwise
  scaling natively and always returns a sparse result

## Additional context

- **Affected function:** `sklearn.linear_model._base._rescale_data` (line 220)
- **Affected models:** all linear models that accept `sample_weight` with sparse `X` —
  `LinearRegression`, `Ridge`, `ElasticNet`, `Lasso`, and their CV variants
- **Tests:** the existing `test_rescale_data` in `sklearn/linear_model/tests/test_base.py`
  already covers `sparse_container x n_targets` combinations — no new test infrastructure needed
- **Fix is ready:** branch `perf/avoid-dia-array-in-rescale-data` on fork

## Labels

`Enhancement`, `Needs Triage`, `module:linear_model`


### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Indicate array API support on an estimator's API page
body:
### Describe the issue linked to the documentation

Today I felt like it would be nice to be able to see directly on https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture.html#sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture if it has array API support or not. Having to go to https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/array_api.html#support-for-array-api-compatible-inputs is possible but a bit tedious.

Until today I was thinking that we don't need this because "all estimators will support array API input, even if it doesn't make them faster". Which removes the need to explicitly list it on each estimator's page.

What do others think about this?

title: `partial_fit` for `Ridge`
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the workflow you want to enable

The `Ridge` family is already compatible with the Array API, which is great for pytorch compatibility! It simplifies linear probing evaluations for foundation models. 

However, these models often use datasets with many examples.
It is not always feasible to accumulate features in memory before passing them to a ridge classifier.

It would be nice if `Ridge` had a `partial_fit` method which allowed processing the dataset in batches. 
With $D$ features and $N$ examples, `Ridge` would only accumulate the features statistics ($D \times D$) instead of all the features ($N \times D$)

### Describe your proposed solution

We accumulate the sufficient statistics $A = X^\top X \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times D}$ and $B = X^\top Y \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times C}$, both of size independent of $N$. The closed-form ridge solution is $W = (A + \lambda I)^{-1} B$. Because $X$ is never materialized, memory is $O(D^2)$ instead of $O(ND)$, so the probe scales to arbitrarily large train sets.


### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

Implement it outside of scikit learn: https://github.com/braindecode/OpenEEGBench/blob/ede655f1294feef15ff6be4d6e6d94628ab9187e/open_eeg_bench/ridge_probe.py#L79-L273
Then add a test for consistency with the scikit learn pipeline on a small random datasets: https://github.com/braindecode/OpenEEGBench/blob/ede655f1294feef15ff6be4d6e6d94628ab9187e/tests/test_ridge_probe.py#L42-L99

### Additional context

We are using this for [OpenEEGBench](https://github.com/braindecode/OpenEEGBench), a library for benchmarking EEG foundation models. 

Context: https://github.com/braindecode/OpenEEGBench/pull/32#discussion_r3172514283

title: Make `_array_api_for_tests` accept a `dtype_name` and raise `SkipTest` if the requested dtype/device is not supported
body:
As discussed in #32460, the supported dtype can sometimes vary even if the device name is the same, and we need to do runtime inspection for instance using `__array_namespace_info__.dtypes`. It means that we would need to update all the array API test to pass both `device` and `dtype_name` to `_array_api_for_tests` to do this check and raise `SkipTest` when appropriate instead of running a test that will fail with a low level error message.

This is the case for PyTorch with the XPU device on different kinds of hardware: some Intel GPUs reachable via this device do support the `xp.float64` dtype while others don't.

I think @cakedev0 has access to such hardware on his laptop.

title: Improve clarity of error message in validation utilities
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the issue linked to the documentation

Some error messages in sklearn.utils.validation are very brief and may not provide enough guidance to users.

For example, messages like "Input contains NaN" could be expanded to suggest possible fixes such as handling missing values before passing data to estimators.

I would like to improve the clarity of one such error message to make it more helpful for users.

### Suggest a potential alternative/fix

Improve the wording of existing error messages in validation utilities to provide clearer guidance to users.

For example, instead of a short message like:
"Input contains NaN"

it could be expanded to:
"Input contains NaN values. Please handle missing values (e.g., imputation or removal) before passing data to the estimator."

This change would not affect functionality but would improve usability and reduce confusion for users encountering these errors.

title: Incorrect KMeans centers update
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

KMeans does not produce expected centers update after 1 iteration.

Context: I was trying to use sklearn as a reference to debug a custom kmeans implementation and found this unexpected behavior.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

With `sample_weights = np.ones(...)`, I'd expect `weight_in_clusters[i] == np.sum(labels == i)`, but it's not the case.

Run with `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`:

```
import zipfile

import numpy as np
from sklearn.cluster._k_means_lloyd import lloyd_iter_chunked_dense
from sklearn.utils._openmp_helpers import _openmp_effective_n_threads

with zipfile.ZipFile("data.zip", "r") as zip_ref:
    zip_ref.extractall("data")


X = np.load("data/X.npy")
centers = np.load("data/centers.npy")
sample_weight = np.ones_like(X[..., 0])

K = centers.shape[0]

centers_new = np.zeros_like(centers)
labels = np.full(X.shape[0], -1, dtype=np.int32)
weight_in_clusters = np.zeros(K, dtype=X.dtype)
center_shift = np.zeros(K, dtype=X.dtype)

n_threads = _openmp_effective_n_threads()


lloyd_iter_chunked_dense(
    X,
    sample_weight,
    centers,
    centers_new,
    weight_in_clusters,
    labels,
    center_shift,
    n_threads,
)
print("weight in cluster #996", weight_in_clusters[996])
print("# of assignments to cluster #996", np.sum(labels == 996))

print(
    "expected new cluster #996",
    np.sum(X[labels == 996], axis=0) / np.sum(labels == 996),
)
print("sklearn new cluster #996", centers_new[996])
```

### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

KMeans does not produce expected centers update after 1 iteration.

Context: I was trying to use sklearn as a reference to debug a custom kmeans implementation and found this unexpected behavior.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

With `sample_weights = np.ones(...)`, I'd expect `weight_in_clusters[i] == np.sum(labels == i)`, but it's not the case.

Run with `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`:

```
import zipfile

import numpy as np
from sklearn.cluster._k_means_lloyd import lloyd_iter_chunked_dense
from sklearn.utils._openmp_helpers import _openmp_effective_n_threads

with zipfile.ZipFile("data.zip", "r") as zip_ref:
    zip_ref.extractall("data")


X = np.load("data/X.npy")
centers = np.load("data/centers.npy")
sample_weight = np.ones_like(X[..., 0])

K = centers.shape[0]

centers_new = np.zeros_like(centers)
labels = np.full(X.shape[0], -1, dtype=np.int32)
weight_in_clusters = np.zeros(K, dtype=X.dtype)
center_shift = np.zeros(K, dtype=X.dtype)

n_threads = _openmp_effective_n_threads()


lloyd_iter_chunked_dense(
    X,
    sample_weight,
    centers,
    centers_new,
    weight_in_clusters,
    labels,
    center_shift,
    n_threads,
)
print("weight in cluster #996", weight_in_clusters[996])
print("# of assignments to cluster #996", np.sum(labels == 996))

print(
    "expected new cluster #996",
    np.sum(X[labels == 996], axis=0) / np.sum(labels == 996),
)
print("sklearn new cluster #996", centers_new[996])
```

title: Add 'quantile' strategy to `calibration_curve`
body:
### Describe the workflow you want to enable

Choose between different binning strategies ("uniform" fixed width bins, "quantile" equal mass bins) to estimate the calibration curve.

### Describe your proposed solution

Adding in `calibration_curve` a new `strategy = "uniform"` or `strategy = "quantile"` option to switch between the two binning strategies. We can for now just add the option and change the default in a follow up PR.

We can also add (in the same or follow up PR) the `strategy="isotonic"`  option to implement the CORP approach as advocated in #23132.

### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

_No response_

### Additional context

There are multiple issues / PRs on implementing calibration metrics (for example the expected calibration error, the Brier score decomposition) and improving reliability diagrams:  
#18268, #11096,  #22233, #21774, #23767,  #23132

There are two main ways to estimate the calibration curve (and metrics):
1. using binning, either "uniform" (fixed-width bins) or "quantile" (equal mass bins)
2. using isotonic regression, this is the [CORP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.03033) approach, see #23132

For the binning case, the quality of the estimate in the binning depends crucially on 
1a. the number of bins, it seems that `n_bins=n**(1/3)` is best, this is tackled by #33856
1b. the binning strategy, it seems that "quantile" is better, see [Roelofs et al](https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.08668) or [Nixon et al](https://arxiv.org/abs/1904.01685) 

Reading through the literature, I feel that the "best estimator for the calibration curve/metrics" is not yet settled.   Based on the experiments reported in the [CORP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.03033) paper, the isotonic regression seems the most accurate. However there are ways to debias the binned estimates for the Brier score decomposition that we could investigate, see [Ferro and Fricker, 2012](https://empslocal.ex.ac.uk/people/staff/ferro/Publications/ferro-fricker2012copyright.pdf).

Also, we might want the different options with each its own benefits: fixed-width bins to visually compare different curves or put them in a dataframe, equal mass bins to get a more reliable curve with a manageable length (`n_bins = n**(1/3)`), isotonic regression for its accuracy but at the expense of  a crowded curve (length `n`).

Overall, having the different options seems to me the best way for further implementations of the calibration metrics or curve, without having to commit to one option over another.

cc @ogrisel @lorentzenchr

body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

`LogisticRegressionCV(use_legacy_attributes=False, refit=False)` errors.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegressionCV
X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
m = LogisticRegressionCV(l1_ratios=[0.0], use_legacy_attributes=False, refit=False, solver="newton-cholesky")
m.fit(X, y)
```

### Expected Results

no error

### Actual Results

```
python3.13/site-packages/sklearn/linear_model/_logistic.py:2135, in LogisticRegressionCV.fit(self, X, y, sample_weight, **params)
   2133 else:
   2134     n_l1_ratios = len(self.l1_ratios_)
-> 2135     self.l1_ratio_ = float(self.l1_ratio_[0])
   2136     if n_classes <= 2:
   2137         newpaths = newpaths.reshape(1, n_folds, n_cs, n_l1_ratios, n_dof)

TypeError: float() argument must be a string or a real number, not 'NoneType'
```

### Versions

```shell
scikit-learn 1.8.0
```

### Interest in fixing the bug

x

title: BUG _parallel_pairwise does not slice Y_norm_squared alongside Y when n_jobs > 1
body:
### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

`_parallel_pairwise` chunks `Y` into slices for parallel execution but passes `**kwds` (including `Y_norm_squared`) unchanged. When `euclidean_distances` is called with `Y_norm_squared` and `n_jobs > 1`, this causes a `ValueError` due to shape mismatch between the sliced `Y` and the full `Y_norm_squared`.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import _parallel_pairwise, euclidean_distances
import numpy as np

X = np.random.RandomState(42).rand(13, 4)
Y_norm_squared = np.sum(X ** 2, axis=1)

# n_jobs=1: no chunking, Y_norm_squared matches Y — works fine
result = _parallel_pairwise(X, X, euclidean_distances, n_jobs=1, Y_norm_squared=Y_norm_squared)
print("n_jobs=1: OK", result.shape)

# n_jobs=2: Y chunked but Y_norm_squared stays full — crashes
_parallel_pairwise(X, X, euclidean_distances, n_jobs=2, Y_norm_squared=Y_norm_squared)
```

### Expected Results

No error is thrown. The `n_jobs=2` call returns the same distance matrix as `n_jobs=1`.

### Actual Results

```python-traceback
ValueError: Incompatible dimensions for Y of shape (6, 4) and Y_norm_squared of shape (13,).
```

### Versions

```
System:
    python: 3.12.9 | packaged by Anaconda, Inc. | (main, Feb  6 2025, 12:55:12) [Clang 14.0.6 ]
   machine: macOS-26.4.1-arm64-arm-64bit

Python dependencies:
      sklearn: 1.8.0
          pip: 25.0
   setuptools: 82.0.1
        numpy: 2.4.4
        scipy: 1.17.1
       Cython: 3.2.4
       pandas: 2.3.3
   matplotlib: None
       joblib: 1.5.3
threadpoolctl: 3.6.0

Built with OpenMP: True
```

Also confirmed on `main` (the code at `sklearn/metrics/pairwise.py` line 1986 still passes `**kwds` unsliced).

### Interest in fixing the bug

Yes — I have a fix ready in #33876. The fix slices `Y_norm_squared` alongside `Y` in the `Parallel` call inside `_parallel_pairwise()`.

title: BUG Spurious UserWarning with the deprecation of penalty in LogisticRegression
body:
When running this minimal example:

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

X, y = make_classification(random_state=0)
log_reg = LogisticRegression(penalty=None).fit(X, y)
```

we get the following deprecation warning:

```
[/Users/glemaitre/Documents/packages/python-stack/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/sklearn/linear_model/_logistic.py:1135](http://localhost:8888/Users/glemaitre/Documents/packages/python-stack/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/sklearn/linear_model/_logistic.py#line=1134): FutureWarning: 'penalty' was deprecated in version 1.8 and will be removed in 1.10. To avoid this warning, leave 'penalty' set to its default value and use 'l1_ratio' or 'C' instead. Use l1_ratio=0 instead of penalty='l2', l1_ratio=1 instead of penalty='l1', and C=np.inf instead of penalty=None.
```

which is completely fine. In my case, I wanted an unpenalized model and thus fix `C=np.inf`:

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

X, y = make_classification(random_state=0)
log_reg = LogisticRegression(C=np.inf).fit(X, y)
```

and I get the following warning:

```
[/Users/glemaitre/Documents/packages/python-stack/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/sklearn/linear_model/_logistic.py:1170](http://localhost:8888/Users/glemaitre/Documents/packages/python-stack/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/sklearn/linear_model/_logistic.py#line=1169): UserWarning: Setting penalty=None will ignore the C and l1_ratio parameters
```

I think that this warning is spurious. We probably set the `penalty` internally which trigger this warning.

title: Incorrect array API namespace and device for DPNP GPU arrays
body: > [!WARNING] > This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label. ### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact When calling array API utilities such as `get_namespace,` `device`, etc. on DPNP GPU arrays, it returns incorrect namespaces and devices. title: _MPLTreeExporter: ValueError when using custom colors
body:
### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

When using _MPLTreeExporter with custom class colors, valid RGB triplets can be turned into invalid Matplotlib color strings if one of the RGB components is `0`.

I found this while preparing teaching/presentation material and trying to make a decision tree use the same class colors as other plots in the same figure.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.tree._export import _MPLTreeExporter


iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data[:, :2]
y = iris.target

clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=42)
clf.fit(X, y)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
exporter = _MPLTreeExporter(
    feature_names=iris.feature_names[:2],
    class_names=iris.target_names,
    filled=True,
    impurity=False,
    node_ids=False,
    proportion=False,
)
exporter.colors["rgb"] = [
    # Use colors with zero components to trigger the bug
    [0, 255, 255],
    [255, 0, 255],
    [255, 255, 0],
]
exporter.export(clf, ax=ax)

# ValueError: Invalid RGBA argument: '# 0ffff'
```

### Expected Results

The tree should render successfully when `exporter.colors["rgb"]` is set to valid RGB triplets, including colors that contain `0` in one or more channels.

### Actual Results

title: DOI badge broken in README.rst (Zenodo SVG not rendering on GitHub)
body:
## Description
The DOI badge in README.rst is currently not rendering on GitHub.

## Badge URL affected
https://zenodo.org/badge/21369/scikit-learn/scikit-learn.svg

## Root Cause
As of early 2026, Zenodo SVG badges no longer render inline on GitHub pages, 
though the SVG links still work if accessed directly.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Visit https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn
2. Observe the broken DOI badge image in the badge row

## Environment
- Checked on: April 2026
- Browser: Google Chrome

### Suggest a potential alternative/fix

## Suggested Fix
Replace the Zenodo badge with a shields.io equivalent that renders correctly:

.. |DOI| image:: https://img.shields.io/badge/DOI-10.5281%2Fzenodo-blue.svg
   :target: https://zenodo.org/badge/latestdoi/21369/scikit-learn/scikit-learn

title: `pairwise_distances` throws warnings with array API classes
body:
### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

According to the docs:
https://scikit-learn.org/dev/modules/array_api.html

`pairwise_distances` should have support for array API for metrics like euclidean.

But it appears to still rely on functions with numpy-specific code:
https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/ad136afbe4d316f219d8d770d15a76a1b5e9cd05/sklearn/utils/extmath.py#L219

.. which leads it to throw warnings when used with array API (see code snippet).

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
import os
os.environ["SCIPY_ARRAY_API"] = "1"
import numpy as np
import array_api_strict
from sklearn import config_context
from sklearn.metrics import pairwise_distances
rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=123)

X = rng.standard_normal(size=(5,5))
Y = rng.standard_normal(size=(5,5))

Xa = array_api_strict.from_dlpack(X)
Ya = array_api_strict.from_dlpack(Y)
with config_context(array_api_dispatch=True):
    pairwise_distances(Xa, Ya)
```

### Expected Results

No warning.

### Actual Results

```
/localdisk2/mkl/dcortes/repos/skl18-torch19/sklearn/utils/extmath.py:219: UserWarning: You are comparing a array_api_strict dtype against a NumPy native dtype object, but you probably don't want to do this. array_api_strict dtype objects compare unequal to their NumPy equivalents. Such cross-library comparison is not supported by the standard.
  and a.dtype in (np.float32, np.float64)
```

title: `.score()` with array API does not follow same 'everything follows X' logic as `.fit()`
body:
### Describe the bug and give evidence about its user-facing impact

The logic for array API was recently modified to allow 'y' inputs of a potentially different type than 'X', particularly when it comes to classifiers where 'y' might be strings.

But it appears this logic does not always work when `y` is a pandas object, or at least doesn't work for all methods.

I guess an easy fix would be to convert pandas 'y' to numpy inside `move_to`, but not sure if by that point it is always passed as an integer-encoded array for classifiers.

### Steps/Code to Reproduce

```python
import os
os.environ["SCIPY_ARRAY_API"] = "1"
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import torch
from sklearn.linear_model import RidgeClassifier
from sklearn import config_context
rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=123)

X = rng.standard_normal(size=(100,10))
y = rng.integers(2, size=X.shape[0])

Xt = torch.tensor(X)
ys = pd.Series(y)

with config_context(array_api_dispatch=True):
    model = RidgeClassifier(solver="svd").fit(Xt, ys)
    score = model.score(Xt, ys)
```

### Expected Results

No error.

### Actual Results

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  Cell In[3], line 18
    score = model.score(Xt, ys)
  File ~/code/scikit-learn/sklearn/base.py:615 in score
    return accuracy_score(y, self.predict(X), sample_weight=sample_weight)
  File ~/code/scikit-learn/sklearn/utils/_param_validation.py:218 in wrapper
    return func(*args, **kwargs)
  File ~/code/scikit-learn/sklearn/metrics/_classification.py:407 in accuracy_score
    y_true, sample_weight = move_to(y_true, sample_weight, xp=xp, device=device)
  File ~/code/scikit-learn/sklearn/utils/_array_api.py:602 in move_to
    array_converted = xp.from_dlpack(array, device=device)

AssertionError: device and copy kwargs not supported when ext_tensor is already a DLPack capsule
```

title: ENH: Add confusion_matrix_diff to compare multiple models against a baseline
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the workflow you want to enable

When comparing classification models, users frequently need to understand how one model differs from another on a per-class basis. The current workflow requires manually computing separate confusion matrices and subtracting them:

```
cm1 = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred_baseline)
cm2 = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred_model2)
diff = cm2 - cm1
```

This pattern has no built-in support in sklearn and becomes error-prone when normalization is involved - normalizing before vs after subtraction gives different results and users may not be aware of this distinction.

There is also no standard scalable way to compare multiple models against a single baseline in one call.

### Describe your proposed solution

Add confusion_matrix_diff to sklearn.metrics with the following signature:

```
def confusion_matrix_diff(
    y_true_or_cm_baseline,
    *args,
    precomputed=False,
    labels=None,
    sample_weight=None,
    normalize=None
)
```

**Label mode (precomputed=False):**

```
# Two models
result = confusion_matrix_diff(y_true, y_pred_baseline, y_pred2)

# Three models
result = confusion_matrix_diff(y_true, y_pred_baseline, y_pred2, y_pred3)

# With normalization
result = confusion_matrix_diff(y_true, y_pred_baseline, y_pred2, normalize='true')
```

**Precomputed mode (precomputed=True):**

`result = confusion_matrix_diff(cm_baseline, cm2, cm3, precomputed=True)`

Returns a 3D array of shape (n_comparisons, n_classes, n_classes) where:
- Positive values = comparison model better than baseline
- Negative values = baseline better than comparison model

The normalize parameter mirrors confusion_matrix exactly - normalization is applied to each matrix individually before subtraction, giving meaningful per-class rate differences rather than raw count differences.

### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

Users can subtract confusion matrices manually, but this does not handle:
- Consistent normalization across matrices before subtraction
- Multi-model comparison in a single call
- Input validation for mismatched label sets or matrix shapes

A utility function makes this discoverable, correct by default, and consistent with the existing confusion_matrix API.

### Additional context

- No changes to existing functions — additions only
- Placement: sklearn/metrics/_classification.py alongside confusion_matrix
- Exported from sklearn.metrics
- Full numpydoc docstring and tests included in the PR

Happy to adjust the API design based on maintainer feedback before writing any code.

title: Optimized _class_means in sklearn/discriminant_analysis.py
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

### Describe the workflow you want to enable

This improves the runtime of solves within the Linear Discriminant Analysis class by speeding up _class_means which is called repeatedly. Please note that this is improving an existing TODO on line 122 of sklearn/discriminant_analysis.py.

### Describe your proposed solution

Based on the existing #TODO that is on line 122 of sklearn/discriminant_analysis.py, I found that the performance of _class_means can be sped up by an average of about 17x by switching from bin count and add.at to one hot matmul. Across 24 test cases it improved all of them by factors varying from 4x to 25x speedups depending on the inputs.

### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

I also tried the combo of merge sort and reduceat for chunk sums. This had only slight improvement and sometimes regressed. My proposed solution showed no regressions in my test suite.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: [Callbacks] Add mechanism to realiably re-set `max_subtasks` on `CallbackContext`?
body:
> [!WARNING]
> This issue is not yet ready for a PR. If you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our [contributing guidelines](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html), and in particular the sections for [new contributors](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#new-contributors) and the ["Needs triage"](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/developers/contributing.html#issues-tagged-needs-triage) label.

Not a high priority, but as @adrinjalali hinted and we discussed yesterday in the meeting:

We may consider to add a mechanism to re-set the  `max_subtasks` on `CallbackContext`, so that progress bars can be re-scaled when new information on the maximum number of sub-tasks is available on an estimator or meta-estimator.

Currently, setting the `max_subtasks` after creating a new subcontext, we'd not be sure if this would update the `total` on a `ProgressBar`.

title: DOC: Add explicit mathematical formula for biased standard deviation in StandardScaler
body:
### Describe the issue linked to the documentation

The `StandardScaler` API reference says it Standardize features by removing the mean and scaling to unit variance and mentions `numpy.std(ddof=0)` in the "Notes" section, but does not explicitly state a mathematical equation.

For users with a statistical or mathematical background, it may be less transparent to rely on the documentation details of a third party library like NumPy than to provide a formal mathematical definition, which is easy to interpret as the difference between biased and unbiased estimators may not be immediately obvious to all users.

### Suggest a potential alternative/fix

I propose adding the explicit LaTeX formulas for the $z$-score and the standard deviation to the "Notes" section of the `StandardScaler` docstring. This clarifies the use of the biased estimator (division by $n$) versus the unbiased estimator (also known as Bessel's correction, $n-1$), which might be a point of confusion for users.

I propose rewriting Notes as:

We use a biased estimator for the standard deviation, equivalent to ``numpy.std(x, ddof=0)``, that is:

.. math::

    s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2}



I’d be happy to submit a PR if this approach looks good.

title: Support for building against a SciPy which uses ILP64 BLAS and `cython_blas`
body:
Hi all, this issue is to propose adding support for building scikit-learn against a SciPy install that was built with ILP64 (i.e., 64-bit integers rather than the default LP64 / 32-bit integers - see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/64-bit_computing#64-bit_data_models). 

### Context

SciPy has wanted to have solid/full ILP64  support for years (efforts going back to 2020, and feature requests before that), because there are use cases and algorithms where support for large arrays where array indices don't fit in plain `int`'s is desirable. In the next SciPy release, 1.18.0, this will finally be arriving.

When building from source, there will actually be three builld modes:
1. LP64-only (default)
2. ILP64-only (opt-in with `-Duse-ilp64=true`)
3. ILP64 for all SciPy internals, but LP64 for `linalg.blas` and `linalg.cython_blas` low-level interfaces  (opt-in with `-Duse-ilp64=true -Dcython-blas-abi=lp64`)

See https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/building/blas_lapack.html#bit-integer-ilp64-blas-lapack for details.

Wheels on PyPI will continue to be LP64-only, so the ABI of `cython_blas` and `cython_lapack` won't be broken. However, when building from source, ILP64 can be chosen - and going ILP64-only means that downstream users of `scipy.linalg.blas|lapack|cython_blas|cython_lapack` need to work with that change.

Mode (3) can be useful as a transition mechanism for adopters (and wouldn't affect scikit-learn), but mode (2) is probably preferred. 

### Proposed solution

It is possible for scikit-learn and other downstream users to detect how SciPy was built, and adapt automatically - no new build flag will be needed in scikit-learn, it should "just work". Essentially, `scipy.linalg.cython_blas` will expose a `blas_int` that can be used. It's also not too difficult to use that conditionally when it exists, and fall back to plain `int` otherwise.

Then, there are two ways scikit-learn can go for its own Cython code:

1. Handle the change in [sklearn/utils/_cython_blas.pyx](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/sklearn/utils/_cython_blas.pyx) with a small shim in each function, and keep [sklearn/utils/_cython_blas.pxd](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/sklearn/utils/_cython_blas.pxd) unchanged.
2. Change `_cython_blas.pxd` as well to use `blas_int`, meaning all consumers of that interface (~15 files currently) need to be adapted as well.

It's not an either/or. Option 1 is a very localized change, and should probably be done either way. Option 2 can always be done later - it has the benefit of actually enabling use of large arrays with scikit-learn APIs as well (option 1 doesn't, because `_cython_blas.pxd` still uses `int`). So it depends on whether there's a benefit in doing that.

### Next step

I plan to open a PR for (1) soon. This is fairly well-tested now (https://github.com/rgommers/pixi-sklearn-ilp64), and we are adding a test package to SciPy CI that closely mirrors scikit-learn's `_cython_blas` wrappers. Also, we now have a regression test for Cython ABI/signature changes, so that should be fine now.

If you prefer to do (2), I'd be happy to work on that as well.

text_cleaned:
Support for building against a SciPy which uses ILP64 BLAS and

Hi all, this issue is to propose adding support for building scikit-learn against a SciPy install that was built with ILP64 (i.e., 64-bit integers rather than the default LP64 / 32-bit integers - see

Context

SciPy has wanted to have solid/full ILP64 support for years (efforts going back to 2020, and feature requests before that), because there are use cases and algorithms where support for large arrays where array indices don't fit in plain 's is desirable. In the next SciPy release, 1.18.0, this will finally be arriving.

When building from source, there will actually be three builld modes:
1. LP64-only (default)
2. ILP64-only (opt-in with )
3. ILP64 for all SciPy internals, but LP64 for and low-level interfaces (opt-in with )

See for details.

Wheels on PyPI will continue to be LP64-only, so the ABI of and won't be broken. However, when building from source, ILP64 can be chosen - and going ILP64-only means that downstream users of need to work with that change.

Mode (3) can be useful as a transition mechanism for adopters (and wouldn't affect scikit-learn), but mode (2) is probably preferred.

Proposed solution

It is possible for scikit-learn and other downstream users to detect how SciPy was built, and adapt automatically - no new build flag will be needed in scikit-learn, it should "just work". Essentially, will expose a that can be used. It's also not too difficult to use that conditionally when it exists, and fall back to plain otherwise.

Then, there are two ways scikit-learn can go for its own Cython code:

1. Handle the change in sklearn/utils/_cython_blas.pyx with a small shim in each function, and keep sklearn/utils/_cython_blas.pxd unchanged.
2. Change as well to use , meaning all consumers of that interface (~15 files currently) need to be adapted as well.

It's not an either/or. Option 1 is a very localized change, and should probably be done either way. Option 2 can always be done later - it has the benefit of actually enabling use of large arrays with scikit-learn APIs as well (option 1 doesn't, because still uses ). So it depends on whether there's a benefit in doing that.

Next step

I plan to open a PR for (1) soon. This is fairly well-tested now ( and we are adding a test package to SciPy CI that closely mirrors scikit-learn's wrappers. Also, we now have a regression test for Cython ABI/signature changes, so that should be fine now.

If you prefer to do (2), I'd be happy to work on that as well.
